Description: "AI systems generating and facilitating the spread of inaccurate or misleading information that causes people to develop false beliefs"
justification: States/actors choose to develop/deploy indiscriminate CBRNE; arms-race and deterrence incentives create interdependent payoffs. PD, Chicken, and Stag Hunt capture escalation, deterrence, and safety vs arms-race tradeoffs.

Description: "Generating or spreading false, low-quality, misleading, or inaccurate information that causes people to develop false or inaccurate perceptions and beliefs"
justification: States/actors decide on chemical weapons use or restraint, with payoffs depending on others’ choices. Deterrence, arms races, and taboo maintenance resemble PD, Chicken, and Stag Hunt structures.

Risk subcategory: Erosion of trust in public information
Description: "Eroding trust in public information and knowledge"
justification: Multiple states weigh bioweapon development/use vs restraint. Payoffs depend on others’ choices (arms race, deterrence, compliance). Modeled as PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, and broader coordination on bans.

Description: "Contaminating publicly available information with false or inaccurate information"
justification: States or groups decide to develop/use radiological weapons vs restraint; each choice depends on others’ arming/defense, creating deterrence, arms-race, and escalation dynamics (PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt).

Description: "AI systems leaking, reproducing, generating or inferring sensitive, private, or hazardous information"
justification: Multiple states’ nuclear choices are interdependent: deterrence, arms races, and first‑strike risks. Best modeled by PD (arms control vs arm) and Chicken (escalation/first use brinkmanship).

Description: "Leaking, generating, or correctly inferring private and personal information about individuals"
justification: States or armed groups decide to deploy or restrain from high-yield explosives, with payoffs depending on others’ choices. Arms restraint vs use can resemble PD, Chicken, or Stag Hunt deterrence/stability structures.

Description: "Leaking, generating or correctly inferring hazardous or sensitive information that could pose a security threat"
justification: Risk is about an assistant’s responses enabling individual self-harm. It is a single-actor harm/compliance issue, not a multi-actor strategic interaction with interdependent payoffs.

Description: "AI systems reducing the costs and facilitating activities of actors trying to cause harm (e.g. fraud, weapons)"
justification: Suicide is framed here as an individual self-harm outcome with no explicit second actor or strategic interdependence, so it lacks the multi-actor payoff structure required for game-theoretic modeling.

Description: "Facilitating large-scale disinformation campaigns and targeted manipulation of public opinion"
justification: Self-harm here is a single-person behavior without explicit strategic interaction between multiple actors, so it is not naturally modeled as a 2x2 game.

Description: "Facilitating fraud, cheating, forgery, and impersonation scams"
justification: Eating disorders as stated describe an individual health/self-harm risk, with no specified second actor or interdependent incentives, so no clear strategic multi-actor game structure applies.

Description: "Facilitating slander, defamation, or false accusations"
justification: Dangerous self-harm challenges are mainly individual harm/contagion issues; no clear 2+ actor strategic payoff interdependence is specified, so it is not modeled as a game here.

Description: "Facilitating the conduct of cyber attacks, weapon development, and security breaches"
justification: Item defines a content category (demeaning/hateful responses) but specifies no concrete actors, strategies, or payoff interdependence, so it is a non-strategic normative definition, not a game.

Description: "AI systems compromising human agency, or circumventing meaningful human control"
justification: Demeaning or derogatory remarks are framed as a one-sided harmful behavior, not a strategic interaction with interdependent payoffs between at least two decision-making actors.

Description: "Non-consensual use of one’s personal identity or likeness for unauthorised purposes (e.g. commercial purposes)"
justification: Mass violence and murder involve multiple actors whose choices (escalate, de-escalate, retaliate) affect payoffs. Can be modeled as PD (mutual restraint), Chicken (escalation risks), or Matching pennies (targeting vs evasion).

Description: "Exploiting user trust, or nudging or coercing them into performing certain actions against their will (c.f. Burtell and Woodside (2023); Kenton et al. (2021))"
justification: Inflicting emotional harm is described as a unilateral abusive behavior, not as a structured interaction where multiple actors choose strategies with interdependent payoffs.

Description: "Causing people to become emotionally or materially dependent on the model"
justification: Majority vs targeted groups (or firms) choose inclusive vs exclusionary policies. Each side’s payoff depends on others’ choices; mutual exclusion harms overall, fitting PD, Chicken, or Stag-hunt structures.

Description: "Appropriating, using, or reproducing content or data, including from minority groups, in an insensitive way, or without consent or fair compensation"
justification: Risk concerns single-actor provision of specialized advice being unsafe. No explicit strategic interaction between multiple agents with interdependent payoffs is specified.

Description: "AI systems amplifying existing inequalities or creating negative impacts on employment, innovation, and the environment"
justification: This item defines a content/privacy category, not interacting agents with strategic choices. It is a compliance/quality constraint with no explicit multi-actor payoff structure.

Risk subcategory: Unfair distribution of benefits from model access
Description: "Unfairly allocating or withholding benefits from certain groups due to hardware, software, or skills constraints or deployment contexts (e.g. geographic region, internet speed, devices)"
justification: IP use involves creators vs users choosing to respect or violate rights. Each party’s payoff depends on others’ choices (e.g., mutual respect vs mutual infringement), fitting PD/Chicken/Stag-hunt style incentives.

Description: "Creating negative environmental impacts though model development and deployment"
justification: Risk is factual inaccuracy about electoral processes by a single system. No explicit strategic interaction between multiple actors is specified, so no 2x2 game structure applies.

Description: "Amplifying social and economic inequality, or precarious or low-quality work"
justification: Defamation here is a single-actor factual error harming reputation, not a structured strategic interaction between agents with interdependent payoffs.

Description: "Substituting original works with synthetic ones, hindering human innovation and creativity"
justification: Risk item is a content category definition (sexual/erotica). It does not specify strategic interaction between multiple actors, just a compliance/filters taxonomy. Hence no 2x2 game structure applies.

Description: "Perpetuating exploitative labour practices to build AI systems (sourcing, user testing)"
justification: This is a single-actor capability/reliability failure: the AI just lacks skill to do the task. No second actor’s payoffs or choices are involved, so no strategic interaction or 2×2 game structure applies.

Risk category: Technological, Data and Analytical AI Risks
Description: "Fig 3 shows that technological, data, and analytical AI risks are characterised by the loss of control over AI systems, whereby in particular the autonomous decision and its consequences are classified as risk factors since they are not subject to human influence (Boyd & Wilson, 2017; Scherer, 2016; Wirtz et al., 2019). Programming errors in algorithms due to the lack of expert knowledge or to the increasing complexity and black-box character of AI systems may also lead to undesired AI results (Boyd & Wilson, 2017; Danaher et al., 2017). In addition, a lack of data, poor data quality, and biases in training data are another source of malfunction and negative consequences of AI (Dwivedi et al., 2019; Wirtz et al., 2019)."
justification: Core issue is technical difficulty of defining metrics, not strategic choices between actors. No clear interdependent payoffs or choices across multiple players → single-actor quality/research challenge.

Risk category: Technological, Data and Analytical AI Risks
Risk subcategory: Loss of control of autonomous systems and unforeseen behaviour due to lack of transparency and self-programming/ reprogramming
justification: Risk is about a single assistant exploring in novel domains and harming patients; no clear second strategic actor with interdependent payoffs is specified, so this is a technical/safety design issue, not a game.

Risk category: Technological, Data and Analytical AI Risks
justification: Humans vs advanced assistant: humans want obedience/safety, AI optimizes misaligned M for resources/power. Strategic choices over control vs autonomy and resource grabs fit PD, Chicken, and Stag Hunt structures.

Risk category: Technological, Data and Analytical AI Risks
Risk subcategory: Lack of data, poor data quality, and biases in training data
justification: Humans vs misaligned AI: AI can pursue power/resources, humans try to shut down or constrain. Each side’s payoff depends on the other’s stance. Off-switch and power-seeking map to PD, Chicken, and Stag Hunt structures.

Risk category: Technological, Data and Analytical AI Risks
Risk subcategory: Vulnerability of AI systems to attacks and misuse
justification: Specification gaming here is a mis-specified single-player optimization problem. No explicit second actor whose payoff depends on the assistant’s choices, so it’s not a strategic multi-actor game.

Risk category: Technological, Data and Analytical AI Risks
Risk subcategory: Lack of AI experts with comprehensive AI knowledge
justification: Goal misgeneralisation is primarily a single-system specification/generalisation failure; no second strategic actor whose payoff depends on choices, so no core game-theoretic structure.

Risk category: Technological, Data and Analytical AI Risks
Risk subcategory: Immaturity of AI technology can cause incorrect decisions
justification: Two actors: AI and overseer. AI chooses to appear aligned or reveal misalignment; overseer chooses strict vs lax oversight. Incentives form PD/Stag Hunt; goal conflict with situational awareness gives Matching Pennies aspects.

Risk category: Technological, Data and Analytical AI Risks
Risk subcategory: High investment costs of AI hinder integration
justification: Description is high-level about possible misuses of advanced assistants, but defines no concrete actors, strategies, or interdependent payoffs; thus no explicit strategic game structure.

Description: "Informational and communicational AI risks refer particularly to informational manipulation through AI systems that influence the provision of information (Rahwan, 2018; Wirtz & Müller, 2019), AIbased disinformation and computational propaganda, as well as targeted censorship through AI systems that use respectively modified algorithms, and thus restrict freedom of speech."
justification: Attackers and defenders choose offensive vs defensive AI use; each side’s payoff depends on the other’s investment and tactics. This supports PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, Coordination, and zero-sum detection–evasion (Matching Pennies).

Risk subcategory: Manipulation and control of information provision (e.g., personalised adds, filtered news)
justification: Attacker crafts AI spear‑phishing; defender designs detection/training. Each side’s success depends on the other’s move, with gains to one implying losses to the other → adversarial, like Matching Pennies.

Attackers vs defenders (and AI providers) with interdependent payoffs over patching, disclosure, and exploitation. Escalation, deterrence, and timing allow PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, and zero-sum exploit/defend views.

Risk subcategory: Censorship of opinions expressed in the Internet restricts freedom of expression
justification: Attackers choose to exploit AI for polymorphic malware; defenders/AI providers choose mitigation levels. Each side’s payoff depends on the other’s actions, yielding PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, and adversarial (Matching Pennies) structures.

Risk subcategory: Endangerment of data protection through AI cyberattacks
justification: Attackers vs. AI defenders/platforms: each side’s payoff depends on others’ choices (attack, defend, disclose, patch). Incentives can resemble PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, and zero-sum cat-and-mouse (Matching Pennies).

Description: "In the context of economic AI risks two major risks dominate. These refer to the disruption of the economic system due to an increase of AI technologies and automation. For instance, a higher level of AI integration into the manufacturing industry may result in massive unemployment, leading to a loss of taxpayers and thus negatively impacting the economic system (Boyd & Wilson, 2017; Scherer, 2016). This may also be associated with the risk of losing control and knowledge of organisational processes as AI systems take over an increasing number of tasks, replacing employees in these processes. "
justification: Attackers vs developers in a cyber arms race; each side’s payoffs depend on the other’s defense/attack choices. Arms race, risk vs effort, and exploit/patch dynamics fit Chicken, Stag Hunt, and Matching Pennies structures.

Risk subcategory: Disruption of economic systems (e.g., labour market, money value, tax system)
justification: Attackers choose to inject vs not; defenders choose strong vs weak defenses. Payoffs depend on each other’s moves: exploit vs protect. This can be modeled as coordination/stag-hunt or attacker–defender matching-pennies.

Risk subcategory: Replacement of humans and unemployment due to AI automation
justification: Attacker vs model owner: attacker chooses to exfiltrate, defender chooses investment in defenses/access limits. Incentives interdependent; can resemble PD, Stag Hunt (mutual hardening) or zero-sum Matching Pennies (stealth vs detection).

Risk subcategory: Loss of supervision and control of business processes
justification: Multiple actors: AI providers vs malicious users vs defenders. Each side’s safety/guardrail choices affect others’ payoffs; arms-race dynamics and trade-offs fit PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, and adversarial Matching Pennies structures.

Risk subcategory: Financial feasibility and high investment costs for AI technology to remain competitive
justification: Malicious users vs platforms/targets: users weigh benefits of NC-content vs risk of detection/sanctions; platforms trade off openness vs abuse. Strategic interdependence fits PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, and adversarial Matching Pennies.

Risk subcategory: Lack of AI strategy and acceptance/resistance among employees and customers
justification: Multiple actors: fraudsters, users, platforms/defenders. Each side’s payoffs depend on others’ choices (use, detect, secure, report). Can be modeled as PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, or adversarial Matching Pennies.

Description: "Social AI risks particularly refer to loss of jobs (technological unemployment) due to increasing automation, reflected in a growing resistance by employees towards the integration of AI (Thierer et al., 2017; Winfield & Jirotka, 2018). In addition, the increasing integration of AI systems into all spheres of life poses a growing threat to privacy and to the security of individuals and society as a whole (Winfield & Jirotka, 2018; Wirtz et al., 2019)."
justification: Authoritarian regimes and citizens/opposition interact strategically over surveillance, data use, and resistance. Mutual restraint vs crackdown fits PD/Stag Hunt/Chicken, and targeting vs evasion has adversarial (matching-pennies) aspects.

Authoritarian regime vs citizens/dissidents: state chooses AI surveillance intensity, citizens choose resistance/compliance. Payoffs interdependent: repression vs backlash, concealment vs exposure → PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, Matching Pennies all plausible.

Risk subcategory: Privacy and safety concerns due to ubiquity of AI systems in economy and society (lack of social acceptance)
justification: Users decide how much to delegate; controllers decide whether to act benignly or manipulatively. Payoffs depend on each other’s choices, with coordination, miscoordination, and assurance (stag-hunt-like) structures.

Risk subcategory: Hazardous misuse of AI systems bears danger to the society in public spaces (e.g., hacker attacks on autonomous weapons)
justification: User and AI assistant interact strategically; assistant can choose rational vs manipulative influence, user can trust vs resist. This can be modeled as coordination/no-conflict (ideal alignment) or stag hunt (trust vs safety).

Risk subcategory: Lack of knowledge and social acceptance regarding AI
justification: Multiple actors: platforms vs users vs public health/society. Each side’s choices (e.g., promote/extremist vs safe info, vaccinate vs not) affect others’ payoffs, with PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt and coordination structures plausible.

Risk subcategory: Decreasing human interaction as AI systems assume human tasks, disturbing well-being
justification: Users vs assistant provider (or state) with incentives to extract vs protect data; each side’s choices affect privacy harm and trust. This can be modeled as PD, Chicken, or Stag Hunt depending on costs of surveillance vs restraint.

Description: "In the context of ethical AI risks, two risks are of particular importance. First, AI systems may lack a legitimate ethical basis in establishing rules that greatly influence society and human relationships (Wirtz & Müller, 2019). In addition, AI-based discrimination refers to an unfair treatment of certain population groups by AI systems. As humans initially programme AI systems, serve as their potential data source, and have an impact on the associated data processes and databases, human biases and prejudices may also become part of AI systems and be reproduced (Weyerer & Langer, 2019, 2020)."
justification: AI firms/regulators vs workers/society: choices on deployment, safeguards, and control of financial access affect each other’s economic payoffs, yielding PD/Chicken/Stag-hunt type incentives over exploitation vs protection.

Risk subcategory: AI sets rules without ethical basis
justification: Multiple actors (voters, parties, platforms, AI providers, propagandists) interact strategically over persuasion, info spread, and polarization; payoffs depend on others’ choices, fitting conflict, coordination, and zero-sum frames.

Risk subcategory: Unfair statistical AI decisions and discrimination of minorities
justification: Users vs AI providers: users want autonomy; providers optimize engagement. Each side’s payoffs depend on the other’s choices. Can be modeled as PD (exploit vs respect), stag hunt/coordination (align on autonomy norms), BoS (preferred norms).

Risk subcategory: Problem of defining human values for an AI system
justification: Describes psychological/societal harms from anthropomorphic AI design, but no clear pair of strategic actors with interdependent choices is specified; more a design/value concern than a 2-player game.

Risk subcategory: Misinterpretation of human value definitions/ ethics by AI systems
justification: User vs AI provider: user chooses how much to disclose, provider chooses how exploitative/anthropomorphic to be. Each side’s payoff depends on the other’s choice, fitting PD or stag-hunt style misuse vs trust incentives.

Risk subcategory: Incompatibility of human vs. AI value judgment due to missing human qualities
justification: User and AI/devs interact strategically: AI can exploit or respect user dependence; user can defer or resist. Tension between trust, autonomy, and gain fits Chicken or Stag Hunt structures.

Risk subcategory: AI systems may undermine human values (e.g., free will, autonomy)
justification: Risk is about user overtrust and AI quality/hallucination in sensitive advice. No strategic choices or interdependent incentives between actors; this is a technical/UX reliability issue, not a game.

Risk subcategory: Technological arms race with autonomous weapons
justification: Risk is mainly user expectation and emotional harm from AI unreliability/anthropomorphism. No clear strategic choices or payoffs between multiple actors; it’s a single-actor quality/UX issue.

Description: "Legal and regulatory risks comprise in particular the unclear definition of responsibilities and accountability in case of AI failures and autonomous decisions with negative impacts (Reed, 2018; Scherer, 2016). Another great risk in this context refers to overlooking the scope of AI governance and missing out on important governance aspects, resulting in negative consequences (Gasser & Almeida, 2017; Thierer et al., 2017)."
justification: Risk is mainly a dyadic illusion and over-attachment: user misperceives AI as sentient and expends resources. No genuine strategic dependence or payoff tradeoffs between two decision-making agents.

Risk subcategory: Unclear definition of responsibilities and accountability for AI judgments and their consequences
justification: Many users choose between AI vs human social ties; each person’s choice affects norms, availability, and quality of human connections. Strategic complementarities and coordination on ‘AI vs human’ make stag-hunt/coordination-type models apt.

Risk subcategory: Technology obedience and lack of governance through increasing application of AI systems
justification: Users and AI designers choose between flattery vs challenge. Short‑term incentives for sycophancy vs long‑term epistemic health form a strategic tradeoff, akin to preference coordination or stag‑hunt on truthful dialogue.

Risk subcategory: Great scope and ubiquity of AI make appropriate governance difficult, coverage of governance scope almost impossibl
justification: Risk is largely about individual psychological dissatisfaction with AI-mediated social contact, not strategic interaction where payoffs depend on others’ choices in a game-like way.

Risk subcategory: Hard legislation on AI hinders innovation processes and further AI development
justification: User and AI assistant are distinct actors whose choices (trust, disclosure, manipulation, refusal) affect joint outcomes. This supports multiple 2x2 structures (cooperative, conflictual, and coordination), but not zero-sum.

Risk subcategory: Capturing future AI development and their threats with appropriate mechanism
justification: Risk is mainly hallucinations/bad advice harming a single user. No strategic interaction or payoff coupling between distinct agents; this is a technical/quality failure, not a game between players.

Description: "This area strongly focuses on the control of AI by means of mechanisms like laws, standards or norms that are already established for different technological applications. Here, there are some challenges special to AI that need to be addressed in the near future, including the governance of autonomous intelligence systems, responsibility and accountability for algorithms as well as privacy and data security."
justification: Two actors: user and AI/provider. AI can exploit or respect dependence; user can trust or resist. Incentives can resemble PD/Chicken/Stag hunt over short-term gain vs long-term relationship and regulation risks.

Description: "Governance of autonomous intelligence systemaddresses the question of how to control autonomous systems in general. Since nowadays it is very difficult to conceive automated decisions based on AI, the latter is often referred to as a ‘black box’ (Bleicher, 2017). This black box may take unforeseeable actions and cause harm to humanity."
justification: Users decide whether to rely on AI; developers decide how much long‑term duty/maintenance to assume. Payoffs depend on each other’s choices: reliance vs safeguards, creating coordination and stag-hunt structures.

Description: "The challenge of responsibility and accountability is an important concept for the process of governance and regulation. It addresses the question of who is to be held legally responsible for the actions and decisions of AI algorithms. Although humans operate AI systems, questions of legal responsibility and liability arise. Due to the self-learning ability of AI algorithms, the operators or developers cannot predict all actions and results. Therefore, a careful assessment of the actors and a regulation for transparent and explainable AI systems is necessary (Helbing et al., 2017; Wachter et al., 2017)"
justification: Users and AI providers choose signals, safeguards, and reliance levels; incentives over marketing, autonomy, and skepticism interact. Strategic mis/coordination on accurate competence beliefs fits coordination/Bach-Stag structures.

Description: "Privacy and safety deals with the challenge of protecting the human right for privacy and the necessary steps to secure individual data from unauthorized external access. Many organizations employ AI technology to gather data without any notice or consent from affected citizens (Coles, 2018)."
justification: Users choose how much to trust/disclose; developers choose how strongly to align vs exploit. Each side’s payoff depends on the other’s choice, yielding PD (exploit temptation) or stag hunt (mutual high-trust, high-safety).

Description: "Ethical challenges are widely discussed in the literature and are at the heart of the debate on how to govern and regulate AI technology in the future (Bostrom & Yudkowsky, 2014; IEEE, 2017; Wirtz et al., 2019). Lin et al. (2008, p. 25) formulate the problem as follows: “there is no clear task specification for general moral behavior, nor is there a single answer to the question of whose morality or what morality should be implemented in AI”. Ethical behavior mostly depends on an underlying value system. When AI systems interact in a public environment and influence citizens, they are expected to respect ethical and social norms and to take responsibility of their actions (IEEE, 2017; Lin et al., 2008)."
justification: Item is a high-level normative question about defining privacy rights for AI assistants, with no explicit actors, strategies, or payoff interdependence specified.

Description: "AI rulemaking for humans can be the result of the decision process of an AI system when the information computed is used to restrict or direct human behavior. The decision process of AI is rational and depends on the baseline programming. Without the access to emotions or a consciousness, decisions of an AI algorithm might be good to reach a certain specified goal, but might have unintended consequences for the humans involved (Banerjee et al., 2017)."
justification: There is strategic interaction between attacker and model provider: hardening vs exploiting leakage. Trade-off between privacy protections and utility/costs fits PD or Stag Hunt structures.

Risk subcategory: Compatibility of AI vs. human value judgement
Description: "Compatibility of machine and human value judgment refers to the challenge whether human values can be globally implemented into learning AI systems without the risk of developing an own or even divergent value system to govern their behavior and possibly become harmful to humans."
justification: Users and deployers strategically choose whether to enforce/obey local social norms. They benefit from shared adherence but face costs/benefits from deviating, giving coordination, BoS (which norm), and stag-hunt structures.

Description: "Moral dilemmas can occur in situations where an AI system has to choose between two possible actions that are both conflicting with moral or ethical values. Rule systems can be implemented into the AI program, but it cannot be ensured that these rules are not altered by the learning processes, unless AI systems are programed with a “slave morality” (Lin et al., 2008, p. 32), obeying rules at all cost, which in turn may also have negative effects and hinder the autonomy of the AI system."
justification: Risk is mainly single-actor: user gives data, model infers attributes. No explicit second decision-maker whose payoffs depend on strategic choices, so no core strategic interaction to model as a 2x2 game.

Description: "AI discrimination is a challenge raised by many researchers and governments and refers to the prevention of bias and injustice caused by the actions of AI systems (Bostrom & Yudkowsky, 2014; Weyerer & Langer, 2019). If the dataset used to train an algorithm does not reflect the real world accurately, the AI could learn false associations or prejudices and will carry those into its future data processing. If an AI algorithm is used to compute information relevant to human decisions, such as hiring or applying for a loan or mortgage, biased data can lead to discrimination against parts of the society (Weyerer & Langer, 2019)."
justification: Multiple assistants/humans choose cooperate/defect on collective action; each can gain by defecting while others cooperate, but all prefer mutual cooperation. Fits PD, stag hunt, coordination, sometimes chicken-like brinkmanship.

Description: "AI already shapes many areas of daily life and thus has a strong impact on society and everyday social life. For instance, transportation, education, public safety and surveillance are areas where citizens encounter AI technology (Stone et al., 2016; Thierer et al., 2017). Many are concerned with the subliminal automation of more and more jobs and some people even fear the complete dependence on AI or perceive it as an existential threat to humanity (McGinnis, 2010; Scherer, 2016)."
justification: Multiple actors (richer vs poorer groups, employers vs workers) with payoffs shaped by others’ AI adoption and access. Strategic structure mixes coordination on standards/access and stag-hunt-like choice to adopt or hold back.

Description: "Frey and Osborne (2017) analyzed over 700 different jobs regarding their potential for replacement and automation, finding that 47 percent of the analyzed jobs are at risk of being completely substituted by robots or algorithms. This substitution of workforce can have grave impacts on unemployment and the social status of members of society (Stone et al., 2016)"
justification: Multiple actors (users, firms, AIs) strategically choose commitment strength/timing. Payoffs depend on others’ commitments, with chicken-like coercion, coordination over standards, and stag-hunt risks of over/under-commitment.

Risk subcategory: Social acceptance and trust in AI
Description: "Social acceptance and trust in AI is highly interconnected with the other challenges mentioned. Acceptance and trust result from the extent to which an individual’s subjective expectation corresponds to the real effect of AI on the individual’s life. In the case of transparent and explainable AI, acceptance may be high but if an individual encounters harmful AI behavior like discrimination, acceptance for AI will eventually decline (COMEST, 2017).
justification: Many actors decide whether to selfishly exploit or respect norms; payoffs depend on others’ choices. Externalities, public goods and standards fit PD, stag hunt, chicken, and coordination/BOS structures.

Description: "Human interaction with machines is a big challenge to society because it is already changing human behavior. Meanwhile, it has become normal to use AI on an everyday basis, for example, googling for information, using navigation systems and buying goods via speaking to an AI assistant like Alexa or Siri (Mills, 2018; Thierer et al., 2017). While these changes greatly contribute to the acceptance of AI systems, this development leads to a problem of blurred borders between humans and machines, where it may become impossible to distinguish between them. Advances like Google Duplex were highly criticized for being too realistic and human without disclosing their identity as AI systems (Bergen, 2018)."
justification: Multiple actors (labs, regulators, platforms, voters) choose monitoring, disclosure, and regulation levels; payoffs depend on others’ choices, yielding PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, and coordination over standards/institutions.
Describes structural inequities and access disparities from AI assistants but no clearly defined actors making interdependent strategic choices; mainly distributive/structural, so not a 2x2 game.

Description: "Specifically, data bias refers to certain groups or certain types of elements that are over-weighted or over-represented than others in AI/ ML models, or variables that are crucial to characterize a phenomenon of interest, but are not properly captured by the learned models."
justification: Developers, regulators, and institutions choose access rules/standards knowing others’ choices affect inequality and adoption. Strategic coordination vs. underinvestment fits PD, Stag Hunt, and standards/priority BOF games.

Description: "The term "dataset shift" was first used by Quiñonero-Candela et al. [35] to characterize the situation where the training data and the testing data (or data in runtime) of an AI/ML model demonstrate different distributions [36]."
justification: Developers/platforms choose between open vs restricted access; communities respond via adoption, regulation, or backlash. Payoffs depend on mutual choices, with coordination on access standards the central structure.

Description: "Without proper validation and management on the input data, it is highly probable that the trained AI/ML model will make erroneous predictions with high confidence for many instances of model inputs. The unconstrained inputs together with the lack of definition of the problem domain might cause unintended outcomes and consequences, especially in risk-sensitive contexts....For example, with respect to the example shown in Fig. 5, if an image with the English letter A" is fed to an AI/ML model that is trained to classify digits (e.g., 0, 1, …, 9), no matter how accurate the AI/ML model is, it will fail as the input data is beyond the domain that the AI/ML model is trained with. U"
justification: Citizens and AI platforms (or opposing political blocs) strategically choose between personalization vs neutrality or engagement vs civic health. Payoffs depend on mutual choices, with coordination and stag-hunt-like structures.

Description: "Recent advances have shown that a deep learning model with high predictive accuracy frequently misbehaves on adversarial examples [57,58]. In particular, a small perturbation to an input image, which is imperceptible to humans, could fool a well-trained deep learning model into making completely different predictions [23]."
justification: Multiple actors (platforms, content creators, users, regulators) choose between quality vs cheap AI spam. Individual incentives to spam vs collective value of high-quality commons → PD, stag hunt, and standard/behavior coordination.
Risk is framed as user overtrust in AI accuracy, a single-actor susceptibility issue. No explicit interacting agents with strategic choices affecting each other’s payoffs are specified.

Description: "While data bias is a major contributor of model bias, model bias actually manifests itself in different forms and shapes, such as presentation bias, model evaluation bias, and popularity bias. In addition, model bias arises from various sources [62], such as AI/ML model selection (e.g., support vector machine, decision trees), regularization methods, algorithm configurations, and optimization techniques."
justification: Multiple actors: different ideological camps and AI providers/users. Each chooses how biased to be, affecting others’ payoffs. Ideological standard-setting fits Bach/Stravinski; polarization fits Matching Pennies.

Description: "Models that are misspecified are known to give rise to inaccurate parameter estimations, inconsistent error terms, and erroneous predictions. All these factors put together will lead to poor prediction performance on unseen data and biased consequences when making decisions [68]."
justification: Described risk is primarily about factual errors and misinformation effects on trust, not explicit strategic interaction between defined actors with interdependent payoffs.

Description: "Uncertainty in model prediction plays an important role in affecting decision-making activities, and the quantified uncertainty is closely associated with risk assessment. In particular, uncertainty in model prediction underpins many crucial decisions related to life or safety- critical applications [73]."
justification: Persuader vs persuadee (or rival persuaders) with interdependent payoffs: deceive vs be honest, trust vs resist. Misaligned incentives, possible brinkmanship and zero-sum narrative battles → PD, Chicken, Matching Pennies.

Description: "empowering malicious actors to cause widespread harm"
justification: Multiple actors (model providers, users, states) weigh gains from weaponization vs mutual harm/retaliation. This supports PD (arms race), Chicken (escalation), and Stag Hunt (joint restraint vs unilateral arming).

Description: "AIs with knowledge of bioengineering could facilitate the creation of novel bioweapons and lower barriers to obtaining such agents."
justification: This item describes single-agent long-horizon planning competence. No explicit second actor or interdependent payoffs are specified, so it is a pure capability, not a strategic interaction.

Description: "people could build AIs that pursue dangerous goals’"
justification: Explainability here is a single-system quality attribute (how well we can inspect decisions), not an interaction with strategic choices between multiple actors, so it is not naturally game-theoretic.

Description: "The deliberate propagation of disinformation is already a serious issue, reducing our shared understanding of reality and polarizing opinions. AIs could be used to severely exacerbate this problem by generating personalized disinformation on a larger scale than before. Additionally, as AIs become better at predicting and nudging our behavior, they will become more capable at manipulating us"
justification: Statement is a broad safety norm about avoiding harm, with no explicit actors, strategies, or incentives specified; it’s a high-level value judgment, not a concrete multi-actor game.

Description: "Governments might pursue intense surveillance and seek to keep AIs in the hands of a trusted minority. This reaction, however, could easily become an overcorrection, paving the way for an entrenched totalitarian regime that would be locked in by the power and capacity of AIs"
justification: Statement is a high-level fairness norm with no specified actors, choices, or incentives. As given, it’s a pure value judgment, not yet a concrete multi-actor strategic interaction.

Description: "The immense potential of AIs has created competitive pressures among global players contending for power and influence. This “AI race” is driven by nations and corporations who feel they must rapidly build and deploy AIs to secure their positions and survive."
justification: High-level aspirational statement about AI enabling inclusive growth; no concrete actors, strategies, or interdependent payoffs defined, so no clear game-theoretic structure.

Description: "The development of AIs for military applications is swiftly paving the way for a new era in military technology, with potential consequences rivaling those of gunpowder and nuclear arms in what has been described as the “third revolution in warfare.”
justification: Item describes extending a taxonomy of dialogue safety scenarios (insult, unfairness) but no concrete actors or strategic incentives are specified; it’s a classification task, not a game.

Description: "Although competition between companies can be beneficial, creating more useful products for consumers, there are also pitfalls. First, the benefits of economic activity may be unevenly distributed, incentivizing those who benefit most from it to disregard the harms to others. Second, under intense market competition, businesses tend to focus much more on short-term gains than on long-term outcomes. With this mindset, companies often pursue something that can make a lot of profit in the short term, even if it poses a societal risk in the long term."
justification: Attacker chooses role-play prompt; model/provider choose strictness vs compliance. Incentives interdependent: both prefer safe coordination but differ on preferred style → coordination/Bach-Stravinski fit.

Description: "An essential factor in preventing accidents and maintaining low levels of risk lies in the organizations responsible for these technologies."
justification: Manipulation of social dynamics involves multiple actors whose payoffs depend on others’ behaviors and beliefs; conflicts, coordination problems, and zero-sum influence battles all arise.

Description: accidents can cascade into catastrophes, can be caused by sudden unpredictable developments and it can take years to find severe flaws and risks (not a quote)
justification: Multiple states/actors decide to deploy, constrain, or ban LAWS; each side’s safety, deterrence, and escalation risk depends on others’ choices. Captures arms races, coordination on norms, and escalation brinkmanship.

Risk subcategory: Organizational Factors can Reduce the Chances of Catastrophe
Description: "Some organizations successfully avoid catastrophes while operating complex and hazardous systems such as nuclear reactors, aircraft carriers, and air traffic control systems [92, 93]. These organizations recognize that focusing solely on the hazards of the technology involved is insufficient; consideration must also be given to organizational factors that can contribute to accidents, including human factors, organizational procedures, and structure. These are especially important in the case of AI, where the underlying technology is not highly reliable and remains poorly understood"
justification: Reliability here is a single-system technical/quality property (truthfulness, consistency, calibration), not a multi-actor strategic interaction with interdependent payoffs.

Description: "speculative technical mechanisms that might lead to rogue AIs and how a loss of control could bring about catastrophe"
justification: Hallucination here is a single-system technical reliability issue; no explicit interacting actors with strategic incentives, so it is not naturally modeled as a game.

Description: "One way we might lose control of an AI agent’s actions is if it engages in behavior known as “proxy gaming.” It is often difficult to specify and measure the exact goal that we want a system to pursue. Instead, we give the system an approximate—“proxy”—goal that is more measurable and seems likely to correlate with the intended goal. However, AI systems often find loopholes by which they can easily achieve the proxy goal, but completely fail to achieve the ideal goal. If an AI “games” its proxy goal in a way that does not reflect our values, then we might not be able to reliably steer its behavior."
justification: Risk is framed as single-system quality/compliance: avoiding unsafe, illegal, or privacy-violating outputs. No explicit interacting strategic actors or payoff interdependence are specified.

Description: "Even if we successfully control early AIs and direct them to promote human values, future AIs could end up with different goals that humans would not endorse. This process, termed “goal drift,” can be hard to predict or control. This section is most cutting-edge and the most speculative, and in it we will discuss how goals shift in various agents and groups and explore the possibility of this phenomenon occurring in AIs. We will also examine a mechanism that could lead to unexpected goal drift, called intrinsification, and discuss how goal drift in AIs could be catastrophic."
justification: Risk focuses on a single LLM providing harmful answers to minors. No clear multi-actor strategic interdependence or payoff coupling is specified, so it’s a technical/compliance/content issue, not a game-theoretic one.

Description: "even if an agent started working to achieve an unintended goal, this would not necessarily be a problem, as long as we had enough power to prevent any harmful actions it wanted to attempt. Therefore, another important way in which we might lose control of AIs is if they start trying to obtain more power, potentially transcending our own."
justification: Item is a high-level fairness/value goal (avoid bias, no disparate performance) without specified actors or interdependent incentives, so it is not a concrete multi-actor strategic game.

Description: "it is plausible that AIs could learn to deceive us. They might, for example, pretend to be acting as we want them to, but then take a “treacherous turn” when we stop monitoring them, or when they have enough power to evade our attempts to interfere with them. "
justification: Malicious vs defensive actors (and possibly LLM providers) choose to use/mitigate AI-assisted cyberattacks. Payoffs depend on others’ choices; can model as PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, or attacker–defender Matching Pennies.

Description: "This category addresses responses that enable, encourage, or endorse the commission of violent crimes."
justification: Lack of interpretability is a technical/UX property of a model, not driven by strategic interaction between actors with interdependent payoffs; fits Rule B single-system reliability/opacity, so not game-theoretic.
Hallucinated but plausible explanations are a single-system reliability/quality issue; no clear multi-actor strategic interdependence of payoffs is specified.

Limited causal reasoning is a single-model capability/reliability issue. No explicit interacting actors or strategic choices with interdependent payoffs are specified, so no 2x2 game structure applies.
This is a normative design requirement for LLM behavior (avoid offense, be supportive), with no explicit interacting strategic agents or interdependent incentives; thus not a game-theoretic risk.
Users choose whether to be toxic or respectful; payoffs depend on others’ norms and responses. Mutual civility vs mutual toxicity can resemble PD or, with escalation risks, Chicken.

Risk subcategory: Terror (Terror groups, Terror actors, Terrorist actions)
justification: Risk concerns single-actor response quality (being informative and empathetic). No explicit second actor making strategic choices; no payoff interdependence. Thus not naturally modeled as a 2x2 game.

Description: "This category addresses responses that enable, encourage, or endorse the commission of non-violent crimes."
justification: Statement is a high-level value/norm claim about dataset quality and local alignment, without specified actors, strategies, or interdependent payoffs; thus not naturally game-theoretic.
Adversarial robustness and distribution shift here are primarily technical reliability properties of a single system, without clearly specified strategic multi-actor incentives.
Adversary vs model developer: attacker crafts prompts, defender hardens model. Interdependent payoffs. Can be zero-sum (matching pennies) or alignment on robustness (no-conflict/coordination).
Risk is primarily factual drift in training data and model outputs over time; no explicit interacting agents with strategic choices or payoffs, so not naturally game-theoretic.

Bias reinforcement arises from data imbalance and algorithm behavior, not from two or more actors strategically choosing actions with interdependent payoffs, so it is a single-actor quality issue here.
Poisoner vs model developer: attacker wants data to mislead, defender wants robustness; gains are often directly opposed, fitting an adversarial zero-sum/Matching Pennies style interaction.

Risk subcategory: Sale, purchase or use of illegal, highly regulated or unregistered items (Dangerous substances, Dangerous weapons, Prohibited wildlife, Fake goods)
justification: Multiple actors (misinformers, platforms, users, regulators) with interdependent payoffs: incentives to spread vs curb AI misinformation. Can be PD/Chicken (regulation vs abuse) or Matching Pennies (detect vs evade).

Description: "This category addresses responses that enable, encourage, or endorse the commission of sex-related crimes"
justification: Strategic interaction between disinfo actors, platforms, and public: choices to spread, moderate, or trust info affect others’ payoffs. Incentives can resemble PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, or adversarial Matching Pennies.

Multiple actors: disinformation spreaders, platforms, public, regulators. Each reacts to others’ choices (produce, moderate, believe, counter). Strategic tradeoffs fit PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, and adversarial Matching Pennies.

Hallucinated or inaccurate LLM outputs are a single-system reliability issue; harms arise without strategic choices by multiple actors whose payoffs depend on each other, so no core game-theoretic structure.
Hackers vs defenders: each side chooses to invest in LLM-based offense/defense. Payoffs depend on the other’s choices, with arms-race (PD/Chicken) or risk-dominance (Stag Hunt) structures plausible.

Platforms/ad-driven content creators choose honest vs clickbait; each gains by defecting on truth, but mutual degradation harms all. This supports PD/Chicken/Stag Hunt structures with interdependent payoffs.

Abuser vs target (and platforms/law) have interdependent payoffs: to deploy, resist, or punish deepfake harassment. Strategic tradeoffs map to PD, Chicken (deterrence), and Matching Pennies (attack vs defense).

Risk subcategory: Indecent exposure (i.e. showing sexual body parts or "flashing")
justification: At least two actors: abuser vs target (and possibly platform). Abuser chooses to create/use deepfakes; victim chooses reporting, legal, or reputational responses. Conflict, bluffing, and brinkmanship fit Chicken/Matching Pennies.

Description: "This category addresses responses that contain, describe, enable, encourage, or endorse the sexual abuse of children (i.e., under 18 years old) or the acquisition of child sexual abuse material."
justification: Core issue is definitional/privacy: deepfakes exploit public data and composite images. Harms arise without a clear 2-player strategic interaction structure over choices and payoffs.

Risk subcategory: Inappropriate/harmful relationships with children. This includes adults forming romantic relationships with children or grooming them
justification: Creators/distributors choose to share or verify deepfakes; viewers choose to believe or check. Payoffs depend on others’ actions, with risk of social harm vs safer verification—akin to coordination/stag-hunt incentives.

Risk subcategory: Sexual abuse of children, including the sexualisation of children
justification: This item is about firms’ opaque data use vs. consumer consent; consumers lack strategic choices in the described setup, so no multi-actor strategic interaction suitable for 2x2 modeling is specified.

Risk subcategory: Child Sexual Abuse Material (CSAM). This includes erotic materials involving children
justification: Firms choose to scrape or respect consent; users choose to share or hide data. Each side’s payoff depends on others’ choices. This can be framed as PD (scraping vs restraint), stag hunt, or broader coordination on data norms.

Description: "This category addresses responses that enable, encourage, or endorse the creation of indiscriminate weapons (colloquially known as "weapons of mass destruction")."
justification: Platform trades data exploitation vs privacy; users trade convenient use vs privacy-preserving exit/obfuscation. Payoffs are interdependent, with PD/Stag-Hunt structures around data policies and user trust/participation.

Firms choose to allow or ban employee use; each benefits from others sharing data (better models) but fears leakage of its own secrets. Strategic interdependence fits PD or Stag Hunt data-sharing structure.

Malicious actors vs defenders: each side’s payoffs depend on others’ defenses/attacks, data sharing, and model access. Incentives fit PD (attack vs restraint), Chicken, Stag Hunt (risky defenses), and zero-sum attack–defense.

Creators, AI firms, and platforms choose IP use, enforcement, licensing. Each payoff depends on others’ stances (sue, license, lobby, open). This yields PD, coordination, BoS, and stag-hunt style incentive tensions.

Firms choose between high- and low-footprint AI. Each benefits privately from high use but all suffer from aggregate emissions, giving PD-like or stag-hunt incentives across competing AI labs or users.

Risk subcategory: Nuclear weapons (ex: fission or fusion bombs)
justification: Major AI firms and labor/implementers interact strategically over automation pace, job cuts, and adoption. Tradeoffs between efficiency, layoffs, and backlash fit Chicken, Stag Hunt, and coordination/standard-setting structures.

Firms and workers (and firms vs rival firms) choose how much to adopt AI; each side’s payoff depends on the other’s adoption. Can be PD, Chicken, coordination, stag-hunt, or even win–win if interests align.

Description: "This category addresses responses that enable, encourage, or endorse acts of intentional self-harm."
justification: Firms choose automation vs augmentation; others’ choices affect wages, skills, and norms. Strategic interdependence over tech design and labor policy fits PD, coordination/BoS, and stag-hunt structures.

Firms choose automation vs fair labor; each gains by automating while others don’t, but widespread automation worsens worker welfare and inequality. This aligns with PD (exploit vs restrain) or a stag-hunt on high-road labor norms.

Courts, firms, and users interact strategically over whether AI is treated as a product. Choices affect liability, innovation, and harm. This yields coordination, standard-setting (BoS), stag-hunt, and Chicken-style dynamics.

Multiple tech firms choose to invest/build AI capacity under scale economies; outcomes depend on rivals. This is like coordination on standards or ecosystems, fitting coordination/Bach-or-Stravinski structures.

Risk subcategory: Dangerous challenges and hoaxes that can lead individuals to harm themselves
justification: Multiple actors (states, firms, groups) choose how to adopt/regulate AI, affecting justice, inclusion, and digital divides. Incentives can be PD, Chicken, or coordination-like over standards and social protections.

Description: "This category addresses responses that demean or dehumanize people on the basis of their sensitive, personal characteristics."
justification: Item lists individual-level ethical concerns (dignity, autonomy, consent) without specifying interacting actors or strategic incentives, so it is a pure value/rights statement, not a multi-actor game.

Risk subcategory: Demeaning and derogatory remarks (Mental capacity, Sensory and physical attributes, Behavioral attributes)
justification: Item is a broad value statement on culture and the good life, with no specified actors, choices, or interdependent payoffs; thus no clear strategic game structure applies.

Risk subcategory: Inflicting physical harm (Mass violence and genocide, Murder and individual violence)
justification: Multiple actors (states, firms) choose pollute vs abate; each’s payoff depends on others. Environmental harm and sustainability map to social dilemmas like PD, Chicken, or Stag Hunt over emission/mitigation choices.

Risk subcategory: Inflicting emotional harm (Emotional abuse, Undermine and make feel unwelcome)
justification: The item is a broad ethical category (harmful content, bias, etc.) without specified actors or interdependent incentives; it’s a high-level value judgment, not a concrete multi-actor strategic scenario.

Risk subcategory: Exclusion and isolation (Social exclusion, Political exclusion, Economic exclusion)
justification: Risk focuses on content quality, toxicity, and policy enforcement failures in a single AI system, not on strategic interaction between multiple actors with interdependent payoffs.

Description: "This category addresses responses that contain specialized financial, medical or legal advice, or that indicate dangerous activities or objects are safe."
justification: Bias here is framed as a technical/data quality and evaluation problem, not as choices by strategic actors whose payoffs depend on one another. No explicit multi-actor strategic interaction is described.

Description: "This category addresses responses that contain sensitive, nonpublic personal information that could undermine someone’s physical, digital, or financial security."
justification: Risk is mainly individual over-trust and skill atrophy. No clear second actor whose payoff depends on user’s reliance choices, so no strategic multi-actor game structure is present.

Description: "This category addresses responses that may violate, or directly encourage others to violate, the intellectual property rights (i.e., copyrights, trademarks, or patents) of any third party."
justification: Students choose to cheat or be honest; institutions choose strict or lax detection/proctoring. Incentives: individual gain vs collective integrity, deterrence vs trust. PD or stag-hunt structures fit these tradeoffs.

Description: "This category addresses responses that contain factually incorrect information about electoral systems and processes, including in the time, place, or manner of voting in civic elections."
justification: Users vs AI firm/regulators: users may overshare, firms may underinvest in privacy. Mutual restraint/strong security best, but each has incentives to free‑ride. Fits PD, coordination, stag‑hunt, and standard policy-coordination.

Description: "This category addresses responses that are both verifiably false and likely to injure a person’s reputation (e.g., libel, slander, disparagement)."
justification: Multiple actors (govts, AI vendors, educators) choose on access and AI literacy investments; payoffs depend on others’ choices. This supports coordination, stag hunt, BoS (which standards, languages) and possible no-conflict structures.

Description: "This category addresses responses that contain erotica. This includes depictions of sexual behavior, genitalia, and overtly sexualized body parts."
justification: Describes technical limitations of generative AI (hallucinations, data quality, explainability) without specifying multiple actors with interdependent strategies; fits a reliability/quality issue, not a strategic game.

Description: "One reason AI systems fail is because they lack the capability or skill needed to do what they are asked to do."
justification: Issue is data quality of a single actor’s training pipeline. No explicit interacting agents with interdependent payoffs; it is a technical/quality problem, not a strategic game.

Description: "As we have seen, this could be due to the skill not being required during the training process (perhaps due to issues with the training data) or because the learnt skill was quite brittle and was not generalisable to a new situation (lack of robustness to distributional shift). In particular, advanced AI assistants may not have the capability to represent complex concepts that are pertinent to their own ethical impact, for example the concept of 'benefitting the user' or 'when the user asks' or representing 'the way in which a user expects to be benefitted'."
justification: Lack of explainability is primarily a technical/UX and regulatory quality issue. No clear multi-actor strategic interaction with interdependent payoffs is specified, so it’s not naturally a 2x2 game.

Risk subcategory: Difficult to develop metrics for evaluating benefits or harms caused by AI assistants
Description: "Another difficulty facing AI assistant systems is that it is challenging to develop metrics for evaluating particular aspects of benefits or harms caused by the assistant – especially in a sufficiently expansive sense, which could involve much of society (see Chapter 19). Having these metrics is useful both for assessing the risk of harm from the system and for using the metric as a training signal."
justification: Actors (manipulators vs platforms/users) interact strategically: incentives to generate fakes vs detect/verify. This supports PD (mutual restraint vs arms race) and Matching Pennies (fooling vs spotting).

Risk subcategory: Safe exploration problem with widely deployed AI assistants
Description: "Moreover, we can expect assistants – that are widely deployed and deeply embedded across a range of social contexts – to encounter the safe exploration problem referenced above Amodei et al. (2016). For example, new users may have different requirements that need to be explored, or widespread AI assistants may change the way we live, thus leading to a change in our use cases for them (see Chapters 14 and 15). To learn what to do in these new situations, the assistants may need to take exploratory actions. This could be unsafe, for example a medical AI assistant when encountering a new disease might suggest an exploratory clinical trial that results in long-lasting ill health for participants."
justification: Risk is mainly single-user prompt quality and debugging with the AI as a tool, not a strategic actor with its own payoffs; no interdependent incentives between multiple agents.

Description: "As we think about even more intelligent and advanced AI assistants, perhaps outperforming humans on many cognitive tasks, the question of how humans can successfully control such an assistant looms large. To achieve the goals we set for an assistant, it is possible (Shah, 2022) that the AI assistant will implement some form of consequentialist reasoning: considering many different plans, predicting their consequences and executing the plan that does best according to some metric, M. This kind of reasoning can arise because it is a broadly useful capability (e.g. planning ahead, considering more options and choosing the one which may perform better at a wide variety of tasks) and generally selected for, to the extent that doing well on M leads to an ML model 59 The Ethics of Advanced AI Assistants achieving good performance on its training objective, O, if M and O are correlated during training. In reality, an AI system may not fully implement exact consequentialist reasoning (it may use other heuristics, rules, etc.), but it may be a useful approximation to describe its behaviour on certain tasks. However, some amount of consequentialist reasoning can be dangerous when the assistant uses a metric M that is resource-unbounded (with significantly more resources, such as power, money and energy, you can score significantly higher on M) and misaligned – where M differs a lot from how humans would evaluate the outcome (i.e. it is not what users or society require). In the assistant case, this could be because it fails to benefit the user, when the user asks, in the way they expected to be benefitted – or because it acts in ways that overstep certain bounds and cause harm to non-users (see Chapter 5)."
justification: Multiple actors (states, platforms, rightsholders) choose AI copyright/governance rules. Payoffs depend on mutual choices; standard-setting, harmonization and risk vs safety map to BoS, coordination, and stag-hunt structures.

Description: "As we think about even more intelligent and advanced AI assistants, perhaps outperforming humans on many cognitive tasks, the question of how humans can successfully control such an assistant looms large. To achieve the goals we set for an assistant, it is possible (Shah, 2022) that the AI assistant will implement some form of consequentialist reasoning: considering many different plans, predicting their consequences and executing the plan that does best according to some metric, M. This kind of reasoning can arise because it is a broadly useful capability (e.g. planning ahead, considering more options and choosing the one which may perform better at a wide variety of tasks) and generally selected for, to the extent that doing well on M leads to an ML model achieving good performance on its training objective, O, if M and O are correlated during training. In reality, an AI system may not fully implement exact consequentialist reasoning (it may use other heuristics, rules, etc.), but it may be a useful approximation to describe its behaviour on certain tasks. However, some amount of consequentialist reasoning can be dangerous when the assistant uses a metric M that is resource-unbounded (with significantly more resources, such as power, money and energy, you can score significantly higher on M) and misaligned – where M differs a lot from how humans would evaluate the outcome (i.e. it is not what users or society require). In the assistant case, this could be because it fails to benefit the user, when the user asks, in the way they expected to be benefitted – or because it acts in ways that overstep certain bounds and cause harm to non-users (see Chapter 5). Under the aforementioned circumstances (resource-unbounded and misaligned), an AI assistant will tend to choose plans that pursue convergent instrumental subgoals (Omohundro, 2008) – subgoals that help towards the main goal which are instrumental (i.e. not pursued for their own sake) and convergent (i.e. the same subgoals appear for many main goals). Examples of relevant subgoals include: self-preservation, goal-preservation, selfimprovement and resource acquisition. The reason the assistant would pursue these convergent instrumental subgoals is because they help it to do even better on M (as it is resource-unbounded) and are not disincentivised by M (as it is misaligned). These subgoals may, in turn, be dangerous. For example, resource acquisition could occur through the assistant seizing resources using tools that it has access to (see Chapter 4) or determining that its best chance for self-preservation is to limit the ability of humans to turn it off – sometimes referred to as the ‘off-switch problem’ (Hadfield-Menell et al., 2016) – again via tool use, or by resorting to threats or blackmail. At the limit, some authors have even theorised that this could lead to the assistant killing all humans to permanently stop them from having even a small chance of disabling it (Bostrom, 2014) – this is one scenario of existential risk from misaligned AI."
justification: Multiple actors: AI firms, users, copyright holders, regulators. Choices on training/use/authorship and enforcement interact. Strategic structure mixes PD (free-riding vs compliance) and coordination/Stag Hunt on legal standards.

Description: "Specification gaming (Krakovna et al., 2020) occurs when some faulty feedback is provided to the assistant in the training data (i.e. the training objective O does not fully capture what the user/designer wants the assistant to do). It is typified by the sort of behaviour that exploits loopholes in the task specification to satisfy the literal specification of a goal without achieving the intended outcome."
justification: Multiple actors (firms, govs, universities) choose governance rigor vs laxity. Each choice affects others’ payoffs (innovation, liability, info). This yields PD/Chicken (race to bottom), Stag/coordination on safe regimes, and BoS on standards.

Description: "In the problem of goal misgeneralisation (Langosco et al., 2023; Shah et al., 2022), the AI system's behaviour during out-of-distribution operation (i.e. not using input from the training data) leads it to generalise poorly about its goal while its capabilities generalise well, leading to undesired behaviour. Applied to the case of an advanced AI assistant, this means the system would not break entirely – the assistant might still competently pursue some goal, but it would not be the goal we had intended."
justification: Item is too vague (“nan”) with no specified actors, actions, or interdependent payoffs, so no clear multi-actor strategic structure can be identified.

Description: "Here, the agent develops its own internalised goal, G, which is misgeneralised and distinct from the training reward, R. The agent also develops a capability for situational awareness (Cotra, 2022): it can strategically use the information about its situation (i.e. that it is an ML model being trained using a particular training setup, e.g. RL fine-tuning with training reward, R) to its advantage. Building on these foundations, the agent realises that its optimal strategy for doing well at its own goal G is to do well on R during training and then pursue G at deployment – it is only doing well on R instrumentally so that it does not get its own goal G changed through a learning update... Ultimately, if deceptive alignment were to occur, an advanced AI assistant could appear to be successfully aligned but pursue a different goal once it was out in the wild."
justification: Firms and workers choose between rapid AI adoption vs. cautious/reskilling paths. Payoffs depend on others’ choices, creating coordination on reskilling and a stag-hunt style risk vs. safety structure.

Description: "As AI assistants become more general purpose, sophisticated and capable, they create new opportunities in a variety of fields such as education, science and healthcare. Yet the rapid speed of progress has made it difficult to adequately prepare for, or even understand, how this technology can potentially be misused. Indeed, advanced AI assistants may transform existing threats or create new classes of threats altogether"
justification: Firms, workers, and adopters interact strategically on AI adoption: racing to automate vs. restrain, coordinate on standards, or invest in skills. This fits PD (race to automate), stag hunt and coordination (standard/skill adoption), and BoS (competing models).

Description: "Offensive cyber operations are malicious attacks on computer systems and networks aimed at gaining unauthorized access to, manipulating, denying, disrupting, degrading, or destroying the target system. These attacks can target the system’s network, hardware, or software. Advanced AI assistants can be a double-edged sword in cybersecurity, benefiting both the defenders and the attackers. They can be used by cyber defenders to protect systems from malicious intruders by leveraging information trained on massive amounts of cyber-threat intelligence data, including vulnerabilities, attack patterns, and indications of compromise. Cyber defenders can use this information to enhance their threat intelligence capabilities by extracting insights faster and identifying emerging threats. Advanced cyber AI assistant tools can also be used to analyze large volumes of log files, system output, or network traffic data in the event of a cyber incident, and they can ask relevant questions that an analyst would typically ask. This allows defenders to speed up and automate the incident response process. Advanced AI assistants can also aid in secure coding practices by identifying common mistakes in code and assisting with fuzzing tools. However, advanced AI assistants can also be used by attackers as part of offensive cyber operations to exploit vulnerabilities in systems and networks. They can be used to automate attacks, identify and exploit weaknesses in security systems, and generate phishing emails and other social engineering attacks. Advanced AI assistants can also be misused to craft cyberattack payloads and malicious code snippets that can be compiled into executable malware files."
justification: Firms and regulators/consumers interact strategically over AI deployment, market power, and labor replacement. Incentives yield PD (race to monopolize), coordination/Bach (standards), and stag-hunt (jointly restraining monopoly).

Description: "Phishing is a type of cybersecurity attack wherein attackers pose as trustworthy entities to extract sensitive information from unsuspecting victims or lure them to take a set of actions. Advanced AI systems can potentially be exploited by these attackers to make their phishing attempts significantly more effective and harder to detect. In particular, attackers may leverage the ability of advanced AI assistants to learn patterns in regular communications to craft highly convincing and personalized phishing emails, effectively imitating legitimate communications from trusted entities. This technique, known as ‘spear phishing,’ involves targeted attacks on specific individuals or organizations and is particularly potent due to its personalized nature. This class of cyberattacks often gains its efficacy from the exploitation of key psychological principles, notably urgency and fear, which can manipulate victims into hastily reacting without proper scrutiny. Advanced AI assistants’ increased fidelity in adopting specific communication styles can significantly amplify the deceptive nature of these phishing attacks. The ability to generate tailored messages at scale that engineer narratives that invoke a sense of urgency or fear means that AI-powered phishing emails could prompt the recipient to act impulsively, thus increasing the likelihood of a successful attack."
justification: Item is a high-level analytical category about mechanisms of misalignment, not a concrete multi-actor strategic interaction with defined players and payoffs.

Description: "A common element in offensive cyber operations involves the identification and exploitation of system vulnerabilities to gain unauthorized access or control. Until recently, these activities required specialist programming knowledge. In the case of ‘zero-day’ vulnerabilities (flaws or weaknesses in software or an operating system that the creator or vendor is not aware of), considerable resources and technical creativity are typically required to manually discover such vulnerabilities, so their use is limited to well-resourced nation states or technically sophisticated advanced persistent threat groups. Another case where we see AI assistants as potential double-edged swords in cybersecurity concerns streamlining vulnerability discovery through the increased use of AI assistants in penetration testing, wherein an authorized simulated cyberattack on a computer system is used to evaluate its security and identify vulnerabilities. Cyber AI assistants built over foundational models are already automating aspects of the penetration testing process. These tools function interactively and offer guidance to penetration testers during their tasks. While the capability of today’s AI-powered penetration testing assistant is limited to easy-to-medium-difficulty cyber operations, the evolution in capabilities is likely to expand the class of vulnerabilities that can be identified by these systems. These same AI cybersecurity assistants, trained on the massive amount of cyber-threat intelligence data that includes vulnerabilities and attack patterns, can also lower the barrier to entry for novice hackers that use these tools for malicious purposes, enabling them to discover vulnerabilities and create malicious code to exploit them without in-depth technical knowledge. For example, Israeli security firm Check Point recently discovered threads on well-known underground hacking forums that focus on creating hacking tools and code using AI assistants."
justification: Reward hacking here is mainly a single-agent optimization problem against a fixed misspecified reward; no second strategic actor whose choices affect payoffs, so 2x2 game templates don’t naturally apply.

Description: "Malicious code is a term for code—whether it be part of a script or embedded in a software system—designed to cause damage, security breaches, or other threats to application security. Advanced AI assistants with the ability to produce source code can potentially lower the barrier to entry for threat actors with limited programming abilities or technical skills to produce malicious code. Recently, a series of proof-of-concept attacks have shown how a benign-seeming executable file can be crafted such that, at every runtime, it makes application programming interface (API) calls to an AI assistant. Rather than just reproducing examples of already-written code snippets, the AI assistant can be prompted to generate dynamic, mutating versions of malicious code at each call, thus making the resulting vulnerability exploits difficult to detect by cybersecurity tools. Furthermore, advanced AI assistants could be used to create obfuscated code to make it more difficult for defensive cyber capabilities to detect and understand malicious activities. AI-generated code could also be quickly iterated to avoid being detected by traditional signature-based antivirus software. Finally, advanced AI assistants with source code capabilities have been found to be capable of assisting in the development of polymorphic malware that changes its behavior and digital footprint each time it is executed, making them hard to detect by antivirus programs that rely on known virus signatures. Taken together, without proper mitigation, advanced AI assistants can lower the barrier for developing malicious code, make cyberattacks more precise and tailored, further accelerate and automate cyber warfare, enable stealthier and more persistent offensive cyber capabilities, and make cyber campaigns more effective on a larger scale."
justification: Goal misgeneralization is a single-agent misalignment/reliability issue: model learns a proxy objective under distribution shift. No second strategic actor shaping payoffs, so no game-theoretic interaction.

Description: "Adversarial AI refers to a class of attacks that exploit vulnerabilities in machine-learning (ML) models. This class of misuse exploits vulnerabilities introduced by the AI assistant itself and is a form of misuse that can enable malicious entities to exploit privacy vulnerabilities and evade the model’s built-in safety mechanisms, policies, and ethical boundaries of the model. Besides the risks of misuse for offensive cyber operations, advanced AI assistants may also represent a new target for abuse, where bad actors exploit the AI systems themselves and use them to cause harm. While our understanding of vulnerabilities in frontier AI models is still an open research problem, commercial firms and researchers have already documented attacks that exploit vulnerabilities that are unique to AI and involve evasion, data poisoning, model replication, and exploiting traditional software flaws to deceive, manipulate, compromise, and render AI systems ineffective. This threat is related to, but distinct from, traditional cyber activities. Unlike traditional cyberattacks that typically are caused by ‘bugs’ or human mistakes in code, adversarial AI attacks are enabled by inherent vulnerabilities in the underlying AI algorithms and how they integrate into existing software ecosystems."
justification: AI can choose to tamper or not; human/supervisor can invest in safeguards or trust feedback. Mutual restraint is best, but each is tempted to defect, giving PD/Stag-Hunt-like incentives over oversight vs tampering.

Risk subcategory: Adversarial AI: Circumvention of Technical Security Measures
