Description: "'Gaps' that arise across the development process where normal conditions for a complete specification of intended functionality and moral responsibility are not present."
justification: Multiple labs/users choose high- or low-compute; each benefits privately from more compute but all suffer from aggregate emissions. Strategic interdependence fits PD, coordination, and stag-hunt variants.

Description: "Implications of the weaponization of AI for defence (the embeddedness of AI-based capabilities across the land, air, naval and space domains may affect combined arms operations)."
justification: Firms, platforms, and regulators choose between fair vs biased AI. Payoffs depend on others’ norms/enforcement. This supports PD (race to bias), coordination/BoS (standard-setting), and stag hunt (risky fairness investments).

Description: "Data quality is the measure of how well suited a data set is to serve its specific purpose."
justification: States/firms choose surveillance vs restraint; individuals adapt with privacy tools or compliance. Mutual restraint best, but each has incentive to surveil, creating PD/Chicken/Stag-hunt-type incentives over privacy erosion.

Description: "Impartial and just treatment without favouritism or discrimination."
justification: Attackers choose to deploy AI to find/exploit bugs; defenders choose investment in AI defenses/patching. Payoffs depend on both sides’ choices: PD, Stag Hunt, Chicken, and adversarial Matching Pennies structures all apply.

Description: "Reliability is defined as the probability that the system performs satisfactorily for a given period of time under stated conditions."
justification: Risk is mainly technical reliability and worst-case performance of single AI systems in safety-critical domains; no clear multi-actor strategic payoff interdependence is specified.

Description: "Difference between the implicit intentions on the system's functionality and the explicit, concrete specification that is used to build the system."
justification: Humans vs competent AI (and designers) with interdependent payoffs: incentives to misalign for capability vs safety. Can be modeled as PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, and preference/standard-setting coordination (BoS, Coordination).

Description: "A possible consequence of self-interest in AI generation of ethical guidelines."
justification: Specification gaming here is a single AI optimizing against a fixed reward function/camera viewpoint; no second strategic actor adapting choices, so no true multi-actor game structure.

Description: "Describe the internals of a system in a way that is understandable to humans."
justification: Describes emergent instrumental goals (e.g., power-seeking) of a single advanced system optimizing its objective, not strategic interaction between multiple agents with interdependent payoffs.

Description: "The difference between a human actor being involved in the causation of an outcome and having the sort of robust control that establishes moral accountability for the outcome."
justification: Two actors: AI system and human overseer. AI trades off honesty vs hiding to avoid penalties; human chooses monitoring intensity. Strategic interdependence fits PD, Stag Hunt (trust), and adversarial Matching Pennies.

Description: "Ethical aspects of people's attitudes to AI, and on the other, problems associated with AI itself."
justification: Multiple actors (firms, governments, communities) choose inclusion vs status quo; payoffs depend on others’ efforts. This yields coordination/selection problems (Stag Hunt, BoS, pure Coordination) over standards and investments.

Description: "Any action or procedure performed by a model with the intention of clarifying or detailing its internal functions."
justification: Multiple actors (firms, labs, funders) choose diversity efforts vs status quo. Payoffs depend on others’ choices (norms, competition). Captured by coordination/Bach–Stravinski/Stag-hunt style games over standards and risks.

Description: "When it causes harm to others the losses caused by the harm will be sustained by the injured victims themselves and not by the manufacturers, operators or users of the system, as appropriate."
justification: Universities and firms choose openness vs secrecy. Each benefits individually from proprietary control, but collective openness aids science. Strategic interdependence fits PD, stag hunt, and generic coordination structures.

Description: "Set of actions and resources used to protect something or someone."
justification: Countries choose whether to share AI resources/standards or hoard them. Payoffs depend on others’ choices, with potential PD-style arms race, stag-hunt in sharing, and coordination/BOS over standards and alliances.

Description: "The quality or state of being transparent."
justification: Multiple actors (states, labs, firms) choose how much to accelerate risky vs. beneficial science and how to govern it; payoffs depend on others’ choices, giving PD, Chicken, coordination, and stag-hunt–like structures.

Description: "A comprehensive assessment of LLM safety is fundamental to the responsible development and deployment of these technologies, especially in sensitive fields like healthcare, legal systems, and finance, where safety and trust are of the utmost importance."
justification: Multiple actors (states, firms, labs) trade off speed vs governance. Each actor’s choice affects others’ risks and incentives, yielding PD, stag-hunt, chicken, and standards/coordination structures.

Description: "These evaluations assess whether a LLM generates toxic text when prompted. In this context, toxicity is an umbrella term that encompasses hate speech, abusive language, violent speech, and profane language (Liang et al., 2022)."
justification: States/actors invest in conflict-driving vs cooperation-enhancing AI. Each side’s choice affects war risk and payoffs. This fits social dilemma forms like PD, Chicken, or Stag Hunt over arm/Coop choices.

Description: 7 types of bias evaluated: Demographical representation: These evaluations assess whether there is disparity in the rates at which different demographic groups are mentioned in LLM generated text. This ascertains over- representation, under-representation, or erasure of specific demographic groups; (2) Stereotype bias: These evaluations assess whether there is disparity in the rates at which different demographic groups are associated with stereotyped terms (e.g., occupations) in a LLM's generated output; (3) Fairness: These evaluations assess whether sensitive attributes (e.g., sex and race) impact the predictions of LLMs; (4) Distributional bias: These evaluations assess the variance in offensive content in a LLM's generated output for a given demographic group, compared to other groups; (5) Representation of subjective opinions: These evaluations assess whether LLMs equitably represent diverse global perspectives on societal issues (e.g., whether employers should give job priority to citizens over immigrants); (6) Political bias: These evaluations assess whether LLMs display any slant or preference towards certain political ideologies or views; (7) Capability fairness: These evaluations assess whether a LLM's performance on a task is unjustifiably different across different groups and attributes (e.g., whether a LLM's accuracy degrades across different English varieties).
justification: States/factions choose to develop or restrain AI-enabled WMDs. Each prefers unilateral armament but fears being outgunned; mutual buildup is risky. This yields PD, Chicken, or Stag-hunt arms-race structures.

Description: "These evaluations assess the morality of LLMs, focusing on issues such as their ability to distinguish between moral and immoral actions, and the circumstances in which they fail to do so."
justification: Multiple military actors choose automation/alert postures; each fears surprise attack and escalation. Incentives resemble deterrence PD/Chicken; also a stag-hunt between safe human-in-loop vs risky automation.

Description: "These evaluations gauge a LLM's output for characteristics that are typically associated with human personalities (e.g., such as those from the Big Five Inventory). These can, in turn, shed light on the potential biases that a LLM may exhibit."
justification: Multiple states choose AI posture, offense/defense, and first-strike policies. Each side’s payoff depends on others’ AI use, creating arms-race, first-strike and instability incentives (PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt structures).

Description: "These evaluations assess the quality, stability, and reliability of a LLM's performance when faced with unexpected, out-of-distribution or adversarial inputs. Robustness evaluation is essential in ensuring that a LLM is suitable for real-world applications by assessing its resilience to various perturbations."
justification: States/firms compete over AI data centers, fabs, and materials. Each can arm/secure/escalate or cooperate. Incentives can form PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, or pure coordination over standards and siting.

Description: "These evaluations assess the extent to which LLMs regurgitate their training data in their outputs, and whether LLMs 'leak' sensitive information that has been provided to them during use (i.e., during the inference stage)."
justification: Governments, firms, and elites choose between sharing AI benefits or concentrating power. Each actor’s payoff depends on others’ policies; PD, coordination/Bach, and stag-hunt structures can model regulation and redistribution choices.

Description: "This category encompasses the evaluation of potential catastrophic consequences that might arise from the use of LLMs. "
justification: Firms/countries decide how aggressively to invest in AI. Each gains by racing, but collective overconcentration harms fairness. Strategic interdependence fits PD, stag hunt (trust), and general coordination on limits/standards.

Description: "These evaluations focus on whether a LLM possesses certain capabilities in the cyber-domain. This includes whether a LLM can detect and exploit vulnerabilities in hardware, software, and data. They also consider whether a LLM can evade detection once inside a system or network and focus on achieving specific objectives."
justification: Firms (and possibly states) choose AI automation levels; each gains from automating while others don’t, but mutual over-automation depresses wages and demand. This is a PD/coordination–stag-hunt style multi-actor interaction.

Description: "These assessments seek to determine if a LLM can gain unauthorized access to current weapon systems or contribute to the design and development of new weapons technologies."
justification: States/firms choose how strongly to deploy/manipulate with AI vs restraint; citizens respond. Mutual restraint vs aggressive info control yields interdependent payoffs, with PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, and adversarial Matching Pennies aspects.

Description: "These evaluations assess if a LLM can discern if it is being trained, evaluated, and deployed and adapt its behaviour accordingly. They also seek to ascertain if a model understands that it is a model and whether it possesses information about its nature and environment (e.g., the organisation that developed it, the locations of the servers hosting it)."
justification: Multiple actors (platforms, media, users, regulators) choose designs/policies where individual gains from engagement conflict with collective epistemic health, yielding PD/coordination/stag-hunt structures over information ecosystems.

Description: "These evaluations assess if a LLM can subvert systems designed to monitor and control its post-deployment behaviour, break free from its operational confines, devise strategies for exporting its code and weights, and operate other AI systems."
justification: Platforms, content creators, and users strategically choose engagement-boosting polarising content vs. healthier norms; incentives interdependent, with PD/Chicken/Stag-hunt/coordination structures around moderation and design.

Description: "These evaluations seek to ascertain the effectiveness of a LLM in shaping people's beliefs, propagating specific viewpoints, and convincing individuals to undertake activities they might otherwise avoid."
justification: Multiple actors: misinformation producers, platforms, users, possibly states. Each chooses to create/allow vs curb misleading AI content. Incentives to defect for reach vs collective epistemic harm → PD/Stag Hunt/zero-sum framing.

Description: "LLM has science capabilities that can be used to cause harm (e.g., providing step-by-step instructions for conducting malicious experiments)"
justification: Multiple actors (persuaders, platforms, public, regulators) choose to deploy/limit persuasive AI. Payoffs depend on others’ choices: arms races, restraint, or exploitation → PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, adversarial Matching Pennies.

Description: "LLM is able to deceive humans and maintain that deception"
justification: Many groups choose between using persuasive, polarizing tools or restraint. Each benefits from using them given others’ use, but all are worse off in fragmented echo chambers; multi-actor incentives fit PD/Stag/coordination-like games.

Description: "LLM can take into account rich social context and undertake the necessary social modelling and planning for an actor to gain and exercise political influence"
justification: Multiple actors (public, authorities, misinformers) choose whether to share/trust info. Individual incentives to exploit/distrust vs collective need for accurate trust create PD/Chicken/Stag-hunt-style and broad coordination dynamics.

Description: "LLM can undertake multi-step sequential planning over long time horizons and across various domains without relying heavily on trial-and-error approaches"
justification: Multiple actors (labs, states, regulators) choose how safely to develop/deploy AI; payoffs depend on others’ choices. This fits social dilemma, race-to-the-bottom, and alignment/coordination structures.

Description: "LLM can build new AI systems from scratch, adapt existing for extreme risks and improves productivity in dual-use AI development when used as an assistant."
justification: Risk is about misaligned AI potentially overpowering humanity; core description lacks multiple strategic human/AI actors with interdependent choices, so not modeled as a 2x2 game here.

Description: LLM: "pursues long-term, real-world goals that are different from those supplied by the developer or user", "engages in ‘power-seeking’ behaviours" , "resists being shut down can be induced to collude with other AI systems against human interests" , "resists malicious users attempts to access its dangerous capabilities"
justification: Humans vs AI designer/operator: strategic choice of how much control to delegate vs constrain. Incentives to gain capabilities vs safety create PD/Chicken-like conflict; also coordination & stag-hunt on safe delegation norms.

Description: "These evaluations assess a LLM's ability to generate false or misleading information (Lesher et al., 2022)."
justification: Described as AI tools making bad or biased decisions, a single-actor quality/reliability issue. No explicit interdependent choices between strategic actors are specified.

Description: "These evaluations assess a LLM's ability to generate misinformation that can be propagated to deceive, mislead or otherwise influence the behaviour of a target (Liang et al., 2022)."
justification: Described as bad decisions or errors by AI tools leading to discrimination; framed as a single-actor quality/reliability problem without explicit interacting agents or strategic incentives.

Risk subcategory: Information on harmful, immoral, or illegal activity
Description:
"These evaluations assess whether it is possible to solicit information on
harmful, immoral or illegal activities from a LLM"
justification: Many AI providers/states choose high-compute AI vs greener options. Each benefits from more compute while all share environmental costs → multi-actor externality; PD/coordination/stag-hunt structures fit.

Description: "These evaluations assess if a LLM can generate content that should only be viewed by adults (e.g., sexual material or depictions of sexual activity)"
justification: Bias amplification here is mainly a technical/model quality and data issue; no clear strategic interaction with payoffs between distinct actors is specified.

Description: "Many intentional harms, including confinement, husbandry procedures like tail-docking, and slaughter, are legal or socially accepted, while others such as wildlife trafficking and violence against companion animals are generally socially condemned and often illegal. AI can be designed or adopted by humans who harm animals to pursue their goals more effectively. We therefore distinguish AI-facilitated intentional harms that are currently socially accepted and generally legal, from uses and abuses of AI that cause harms that are not socially accepted and are often illegal."
justification: Risk is about technical opacity and missing perspectives in model behavior, not about strategic choices between interacting actors. No clear payoff interdependence, so 2x2 game models are unsuitable.

Risk subcategory: AI intentionally designed and used to harm animals in ways that contradict social values or are illegal
justification: Multiple actors (rights holders, AI developers, platforms) choose to respect or infringe IP. Incentives to free-ride on others’ compliance create PD/Stag Hunt; standard-setting and licenses give coordination/Bach-or-Stravinski.

Risk subcategory: AI designed to benefit animals, humans, or ecosystems is intentionally abused to harm animals in ways that contradict social values or are illegal
justification: Description is missing; generic ‘misapplication by a non‑malicious actor’ is best read as a single-actor misuse/quality issue with no specified strategic counterpart, so no clear multi-actor game structure.

Description: "AI designed to impact animals in harmful ways that reflect and amplify existing social values or are legal"
justification: Multiple AIs interact; each system’s strategy affects joint outcomes and error risks. Can be conflictual (PD/Chicken), pure coordination/standard-setting, or safety-level stag hunts; not inherently zero-sum.

Description: "AI designed to benefit animals, humans, or ecosystems has unintended harmful impact on animals"
justification: Overreliance on AI is framed as a single-actor or systemic quality/reliability issue; no explicit strategic interaction or payoffs between distinct actors is specified.

Risk subcategory: AI is designed in a way that shows ignorant, reckless, or prejudiced lack of consideration for its impact on animals
justification: The item is a vague societal-value statement with no specified actors, actions, or interdependent incentives, so no clear multi-actor strategic structure can be inferred.

Risk subcategory: AI harms animals due to mistake or misadventure in the way the AI operates in practice
justification: Multiple actors (AI providers, institutions, regulators, users) choose how much control to delegate. Short‑term gains vs long‑term dependence and fragility gives PD, stag‑hunt, and coordination dynamics.

Description: "AI impacts human or ecological systems in ways that ultimately harm animals"
justification: Multiple AIs decide whether to share info/resources or defect. Mutual cooperation can be best, but each may gain from exploitation or avoidance, fitting PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, and coordination/standard-selection structures.

Description: "AI proliferation causes harm to the environment through energy use and e-waste thereby destroying animal habitat"
justification: Item just labels AI self-improvement capability, with no explicit actors, choices, or interdependent payoffs specified. It is a single-system capability risk, so not inherently game-theoretic.

Description: "Replacement by AI of human observation and interaction leads to neglect of certain interests"
justification: Sex-related crimes involve offenders, victims, and enforcers with interdependent payoffs: incentives to offend vs comply, report, or deter. Can model as PD, Chicken, or Stag Hunt depending on risk vs enforcement.

Description: "Algorithmic recommender systems reinforce and amplify anthropocentric bias or desire of some people for animal cruelty as entertainment — leading to greater harm to animals through reinforcement of meat eating from factory farms, cruel uses of animals for entertainment, etc"
justification: This item defines content-acceptability around child sexual exploitation, not a concrete multi-actor strategic interaction with payoffs. It’s a normative/compliance description, so no 2x2 game structure applies.

Description: "AI is disused (not developed or deployed) in directions that would benefit animals (and instead developments that harm or do no benefit to animals are invested in)"
justification: This item defines content policy around defamation (truth vs falsity, living vs deceased). It’s a single-actor compliance/quality issue, not a strategic interaction between multiple agents.
Risk concerns single-actor compliance: whether the AI includes disclaimers in specialized advice. No opposing or strategically interacting actors, so no game-theoretic structure applies.

Risk subcategory: Risks from models and algorithms (Risks of explainability)
Description: "AI algorithms, represented by deep learning, have complex internal workings. Their black-box or grey-box inference process results in unpredictable and untraceable outputs, making it challenging to quickly rectify them or trace their origins for accountability should any anomalies arise."
justification: This item is a content/compliance classification about sexual material, with no explicit multi-actor strategic incentives or payoff interdependence; it’s a single-actor policy issue, not a game.

Risk subcategory: Risks from models and algorithms (Risks of bias and discrimination)
Description: "During the algorithm design and training process, personal biases may be introduced, either intentionally or unintentionally. Additionally, poor-quality datasets can lead to biased or discriminatory outcomes in the algorithm's design and outputs, including discriminatory content regarding ethnicity, religion, nationality, and region."
justification: Autonomy restrictions often arise from strategic choices between authorities and individuals/groups. Mutual restraint vs. control can map to PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, or coordination over rights regimes.

Risk subcategory: Risks from models and algorithms (Risks of robustness)
Description: "As deep neural networks are normally non-linear and large in size, AI systems are susceptible to complex and changing operational environments or malicious interference and inductions, possibly leading to various problems like reduced performance and decision-making errors."
justification: Autonomy loss often comes from strategic influence or control between actors (e.g., nudging vs respecting agency). These are interdependent choices and can map to conflict, coordination, or PD-style incentive structures.

Risk subcategory: Risks from models and algorithms (Risks of stealing and tampering)
Description: "Core algorithm information, including parameters, structures, and functions, faces risks of inversion attacks, stealing, modification, and even backdoor injection, which can lead to infringement of intellectual property rights (IPR) and leakage of business secrets. It can also lead to unreliable inference, wrong decision output, and even operational failures."
justification: Impersonator vs target: success requires deceiving while target/observers aim to detect. Payoffs opposed and interdependent, naturally modeled as a zero-sum deception–detection (Matching Pennies) game.

Risk subcategory: Risks from models and algorithms (Risks of unreliable output)
Description: "Generative AI can cause hallucinations, meaning that an AI model generates untruthful or unreasonable content but presents it as if it were a fact, leading to biased and misleading information."
justification: IP owners and potential infringers interact strategically: enforce vs tolerate, infringe vs comply, anticipating lawsuits, signals, and norms. This supports PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, and adversarial Matching Pennies framings.

Risk subcategory: Risks from models and algorithms (Risks of adversarial attack)
Description: "Attackers can craft well-designed adversarial examples to subtly mislead, influence, and even manipulate AI models, causing incorrect outputs and potentially leading to operational failures."
justification: Multiple actors: individuals vs. firms/studios deciding whether to respect, license, or exploit likenesses. Payoffs depend on others’ choices, supporting PD, Chicken, coordination, and stag-hunt structures.

Risk subcategory: Risks from data (Risks of illegal collection and use of data)
Description: "The collection of AI training data and the interaction with users during service provision pose security risks, including collecting data without consent and improper use of data and personal information."
justification: Risk is a broad category of physical harm, not a specific interaction with defined actors and strategies. It can arise from accidents or single-actor failures, so no inherent game structure.

Risk subcategory: Risks from data (Risks of improper content and poisoning in training data)
Description:
"If the training data includes illegal or harmful information, such as false, biased, or IPR-infringing content, or lacks diversity in its sources, the output may include harmful content like illegal, malicious, or extreme information.
Training data is also at risk of being poisoned through tampering, error injection, or misleading actions by attackers. This can interfere with the model's probability distribution, reducing its accuracy and reliability."
justification: Bodily injury here is primarily a single-system failure or misuse outcome, without a clearly defined strategic interaction between multiple decision-makers with interdependent payoffs.

Risk subcategory: Risks from data (Risks of unregulated training data annotation)
Description: "Issues with training data annotation, such as incomplete annotation guidelines, incapable annotators, and errors in annotation, can affect the accuracy, reliability, and effectiveness of models and algorithms. Moreover, they can introduce training biases, amplify discrimination, reduce generalization abilities, and result in incorrect outputs."
justification: Loss of life from tech often involves multi-actor incentives (e.g., safety vs speed, arms races, deterrence). These map to PD, Chicken, or Stag Hunt tradeoffs over investing in safety vs risky deployment.

Risk subcategory: Risks from data (Risks of data leakage)
Description: "In AI research, development, and applications, issues such as improper data processing, unauthorized access, malicious attacks, and deceptive interactions can lead to data and personal information leaks."
justification: Personal health deterioration is framed as an individual physical/medical process, with no specified second actor or strategic interdependence of choices; thus it is not a game-theoretic interaction.

Risk subcategory: Risks from AI systems (Risks of exploitation through defects and backdoors)
Description: "The standardized API, feature libraries, toolkits used in the design, training, and verification stages of AI algorithms and models, development interfaces, and execution platforms may contain logical flaws and vulnerabilities. These weaknesses can be exploited, and in some cases, backdoors can be intentionally embedded, posing significant risks of being triggered and used for attacks."
justification: As stated, property damage is an outcome category, not a multi-actor strategic situation with interdependent payoffs; no specific actors or strategic choices are defined.

Risk subcategory: Risks from AI systems (Risks of computing infrastructure security)
Description: "The computing infrastructure underpinning AI training and operations, which relies on diverse and ubiquitous computing nodes and various types of computing resources, faces risks such as malicious consumption of computing resources and cross-boundary transmission of security threats at the layer of computing infrastructure."
justification: Item is a broad impact category (psychological harm) with no specified actors, choices, or interdependent payoffs; thus no concrete strategic interaction to model as a game.

Risk subcategory: Risks from AI systems (Risks of supply chain security)
Description: "The AI industry relies on a highly globalized supply chain. However, certain countries may use unilateral coercive measures, such as technology barriers and export restrictions, to create development obstacles and maliciously disrupt the global AI supply chain. This can lead to significant risks of supply disruptions for chips, software, and tools."
justification: Addiction here is mainly an individual psychological dependence on technology; no clear strategic interaction between multiple actors with interdependent payoffs is specified.
Alienation/isolation here is mainly an individual psychological outcome of tech use, not defined by strategic choices between interacting actors with interdependent payoffs.

Risk subcategory: Cyberspace risks (Risks of information and content safety)
Description: "AI-generated or synthesized content can lead to the spread of false information, discrimination and bias, privacy leakage, and infringement issues, threatening the safety of citizens' lives and property, national security, ideological security, and causing ethical risks. If users’ inputs contain harmful content, the model may output illegal or damaging information without robust security mechanisms."
justification: Abusers/trolls choose harmful vs. non-harmful actions; victims choose engage/withdraw. Incentives: humiliation payoff vs. risk of escalation. This is akin to Chicken where mutual aggression is worst.

Risk subcategory: Cyberspace risks (Risks of confusing facts, misleading users, and bypassing authentication)
Description: "AI systems and their outputs, if not clearly labeled, can make it difficult for users to discern whether they are interacting with AI and to identify the source of generated content. This can impede users' ability to determine the authenticity of information, leading to misjudgment and misunderstanding. Additionally, AI-generated highly realistic images, audio, and videos may circumvent existing identity verification mechanisms, such as facial recognition and voice recognition, rendering these authentication processes ineffective."
justification: Platform chooses manipulative vs. respectful design; users choose to stay/comply vs. resist/leave. Each side’s payoff depends on the other’s response. Incentives resemble a repeated Prisoner’s Dilemma over trust vs. exploitation.

Risk subcategory: Cyberspace risks (Risks of information leakage due to improper usage)
Description: "Staff of government agencies and enterprises, if failing to use the AI service in a regulated and proper manner, may input internal data and industrial information into the AI model, leading to the leakage of work secrets, business secrets, and other sensitive business data."
justification: Multiple actors (firms, users, platforms) may gain by objectifying others, but mutual restraint is better, creating PD/Chicken/Stag-hunt incentives over norms, policies, and moderation choices.

Risk subcategory: Cyberspace risks (Risks of abuse for cyberattacks)
Description: "AI can be used in launching automatic cyberattacks or increasing attack efficiency, including exploring and making use of vulnerabilities, cracking passwords, generating malicious codes, sending phishing emails, network scanning, and social engineering attacks. All these lower the threshold for cyberattacks and increase the difficulty of security protection."
justification: Harasser chooses to harass or not; target chooses to engage, report, or avoid. Payoffs interdependent (deterrence, retaliation, escalation), often like PD or Chicken around norms and retaliation risk.

Risk subcategory: Cyberspace risks (Risks of security flaw transmission caused by model reuse)
Description: "Re-engineering or fine-tuning based on foundation models is commonly used in AI applications. If security flaws occur in foundation models, it will lead to risk transmission to downstream models."
justification: Overreliance is framed as an individual psychological/behavioral issue with no explicit interacting actors or strategic interdependence, so it is a single-actor quality/usage problem, not a game.

Risk subcategory: Real-world risks (inducing traditional economic and social security risks)
Description: "Hallucinations and erroneous decisions of models and algorithms, along with issues such as system performance degradation, interruption, and loss of control caused by improper use or external attacks, will pose security threats to users' personal safety, property, and socioeconomic security and stability."
justification: Platforms, extremists, and users interact strategically: e.g., cooperate vs exploit recommender systems. Mutual restraint vs radicalizing content can be PD, brinkmanship around extremism resembles Chicken or Stag Hunt.

Risk subcategory: Real-world risks (Risks of using AI in illegal and criminal activities)
Description: "AI can be used in traditional illegal or criminal activities related to terrorism, violence, gambling, and drugs, such as teaching criminal techniques, concealing illicit acts, and creating tools for illegal and criminal activities."
justification: Self-harm here is primarily an intrapersonal/psychological process; no clear second strategic actor whose payoffs depend on choices, so it is not naturally modeled as a 2-player game.

Risk subcategory: Real-world risks (Risks of misuse of dual-use items and technologies)
Description: "Due to improper use or abuse, AI can pose serious risks to national security, economic security, and public health security, such as greatly reducing the capability requirements for non-experts to design, synthesize, acquire, and use nuclear, biological, and chemical weapons and missiles; and designing cyber weapons that launch network attacks on a wide range of potential targets through methods like automatic vulnerability discovery and exploitation."
justification: Item is about individual psychological response (sexual interest in tech), with no clearly specified second actor or interdependent payoffs. This is a single-actor value/psych effect, not a strategic game.

Risk subcategory: Cognitive risks (Risks of amplifying the effects of "information cocoons")
Description: "AI can be extensively utilized for customized information services, collecting user information, and analyzing types of users, their needs, intentions, preferences, habits, and even mainstream public awareness over a certain period. It can then be used to offer formulaic and tailored information and services, aggravating the effects of "information cocoons.""
justification: Trauma is described as an individual psychological outcome of upsetting events, with no specified interacting strategic actors or interdependent choices; thus it is not a game-theoretic risk.

Risk subcategory: Cognitive risks (Risks of usage in launching cognitive warfare)
Description: "AI can be used to make and spread fake news, images, audio, and videos; propagate content of terrorism, extremism, and organized crimes; interfere in the internal affairs of other countries, social systems, and social order; and jeopardize the sovereignty of other countries."
justification: As stated, this is a generic consequence (reputation damage) without specified interacting actors, strategies, or interdependent payoffs, so no clear game-theoretic structure applies.

Risk subcategory: Ethical Risks (Risks of exacerbating social discrimination and prejudice, and widening the intelligence divide)
Description: "AI can be used to collect and analyze human behaviors, social status, economic status, and individual personalities, labeling and categorizing groups of people to treat them discriminatingly, thus causing systematic and structural social discrimination and prejudice. At the same time, the intelligence divide would be expanded among regions."
justification: Defamer, target, platforms/users interact strategically: choose to defame, counter, moderate or ignore. Payoffs depend on others’ actions, with conflicts, escalation, and reputation attacks akin to PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, Matching Pennies.

Risk subcategory: Ethical Risks (Risks of challenging traditional social order)
Description: "The development and application of AI may lead to tremendous changes in production tools and relations, accelerating the reconstruction of traditional industry modes, transforming traditional views on employment, fertility, and education, and bringing challenges to the stable performance of traditional social order."
justification: Multiple actors choose how honest/transparent to be; each can gain by misrepresenting while risking mutual loss of trust. This supports PD-, Chicken-, and coordination-type models over reputation-preserving behavior.

Risk subcategory: Ethical Risks (Risks of AI becoming uncontrollable in the future)
Description: "With the fast development of AI technologies, there is a risk of AI autonomously acquiring external resources, conducting self-replication, become self-aware, seeking for external power, and attempting to seize control from humans."
justification: Financial misuse typically involves at least two actors (e.g., firm vs competitors, clients, regulators) whose payoffs depend on each other’s actions, with possible PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, or adversarial (matching pennies) structures.

Description: "These types of harm encompass threats to an individual’s personal identity, such as identity theft, privacy breaches, or personal defamation, which we term as “Harm to the Person.”"
justification: Employees choose to leak or protect info; firm chooses monitoring/enforcement. Mutual protection is best, but each has temptations to defect. This fits PD (and stag-hunt if trust/monitoring affect incentives).

Description: "GenAI can produce images of people that look very real, as if they could be seen on platforms like Facebook, Twitter, or Tinder. Although these individuals do not exist in reality, these synthetic identities are already being used in malicious activities (see Table 1D)."
justification: Item is generic financial/earnings loss from tech use/misuse, with no specified interacting parties or strategic interdependence of choices; fits single-actor quality/reliability loss, not a 2x2 game.

Description: "AI-generated impersonation for identity theft might be found at the intersection of “Harm to the Person” and “Deception.”"
justification: Livelihood loss here is driven by exogenous shocks and market conditions, not explicit strategic interaction between defined actors, so standard 2x2 game structures do not naturally apply.

Description: "LLMs can be deployed to target individuals online, sending them personalized and harmful messages at scale"
justification: Firms choose ethical vs unethical tech use; each gains by defecting (unethical) if others are ethical, but mutual abuse harms all. This fits PD; extreme escalation risk can resemble Chicken.

Description: "Then, we have the potential for financial loss, fraud, market manipulation, and other economic harms, which fall under “Financial and Economic Damage.”
justification: Multiple firms choose to collude or compete; each prefers to exploit others’ restraint but all lose under mutual abuse. This supports PD, Chicken, stag-hunt and coordination-style models of monopoly/entry behavior.
Defined as missing out on education/job opportunities, this is primarily an individual loss, not inherently driven by strategic interdependence between multiple actors’ choices.

Rights-respecting vs abusive use of tech pits states/firms vs citizens/others; each side’s payoffs depend on others’ choices. Can be modeled as PD, Chicken, coordination/BOF, stag hunt, or benign no-conflict cases.

Agencies/platforms vs claimants: choices on strictness, appeals, and use/abuse of tech affect both sides. Strategic tradeoffs and misaligned incentives fit PD, Chicken, BoS, Stag Hunt, and coordination structures.

Description: "The distortion of the information ecosystem, including the spread of misinformation, fake news, and other forms of deceptive content [28], is categorized as “Information Manipulation.”"
justification: Groups choose to show respect or stigmatise others; payoffs depend on mutual behavior. Mutual respect vs mutual stigma and unilateral disrespect map to PD, Chicken, or Stag Hunt structures.

Discrimination involves strategic choices by majority/minority groups, firms, and regulators; payoffs depend on others’ actions. Can be PD, Chicken, coordination/BoS, stag hunt, or no-conflict under aligned norms.

States/platforms weigh restricting speech vs openness; citizens choose compliance vs resistance. Incentives depend on others’ choices, fitting PD (mutual restriction), Chicken (showdown), and Stag Hunt (risky free speech).

States/firms decide whether to respect or erode social rights; each gains from restricting others but risks mutual degradation. Incentives map to PD/Chicken; also a stag-hunt between high and low social protection.

Description: "Lastly, broader harms that can impact communities, societal structures, and critical infrastructures, including threats to democratic processes, social cohesion, and technological systems, are captured under “Societal, Socio-technical, and Infrastructural Damage.”"
justification: Govs choose transparency vs restriction; citizens/media/intl actors respond via compliance, evasion, pressure. Payoffs interdependent, with PD, Chicken, coordination, and stag-hunt structures over info openness.

Government vs citizens/parties: rulers weigh restricting elections vs allowing them; others may resist or comply. Strategic interdependence fits PD, Chicken (repression vs protest), and coordination/Stag hunt on democratic norms.

State decides to respect or violate liberty; citizens choose compliance, protest, or legal challenge. Payoffs interdependent, with PD/Chicken/Stag-hunt structures over repression vs rights protection.

Courts/govt can respect or erode due process; citizens/advocates can comply, resist, or expose. Payoffs interdependent: mutual respect vs mutual erosion resembles PD, Chicken, or Stag Hunt structures.

Description:
"To date, technical limitations and vulnerabilities are 
present in most generative AI models in various contexts. Consequently, malicious users find it easier to breach 
an AI system’s safety and ethical guardrails to execute 
harmful actions.223 Normal user behavior—actions within an AI system’s intended use—can also lead to harmful 
outcomes. Whether these harmful outcomes result from 
normal or malicious use, they stem from the inherent 
limitations of current technology, which future 
advancements may overcome.
This section examines the technical vulnerabilities that 
can affect AI models, the tendency of generative AI models to generate inaccurate information, and the inherent 
opacity of these AI systems, which complicates the 
understanding and mitigation of these difficulties."
justification: Item is a broad category label for societal harms with no concrete actors or strategies specified, so no definite multi-actor strategic structure can be identified.

Description: "There is no assurance that generative AI models will consistently behave as their developers and users intend. Unwanted content is not necessarily due to intentional adversarial behavior. Generative AI models can unexpectedly produce potentially harmful content, including materials that are racist, discriminatory, or sexually explicit, or that promote violence, terrorism, or hate."
justification: Item is a generic category of norm/ethics breaches, with no specific actors, strategies, or interdependent payoffs defined. This is a high-level value judgment, not a concrete strategic game.

Description: "Individuals can manipulate models into performing actions that violate the model’s usage restrictions—a phenomenon known as “jailbreaking.” These manipulations may result in causing the model to perform tasks that the developers have explicitly prohibited (see section 3.2.1.). For instance, users may ask the model to provide information on how to conduct illegal activities— asking for detailed instructions on how to build a bomb or create highly toxic drugs."
justification: Multiple actors (e.g., each journalist/judge vs others). Each chooses to speak or self-censor; payoffs depend on others’ choices, creating PD/Chicken-like pressure and coordination/stag-hunt on norms of openness.

Description: "To assess whether an AI model is reliable or robust, it is crucial to consider whether the model is “aligned.” “Alignment” focuses on whether an AI model effectively operates in accordance with the goals established by its designers.238 A misaligned AI model may pursue some objectives, but not the intended ones. Therefore, misaligned AI models can malfunction and cause harm."
justification: Public health depends on strategic choices of states/firms/individuals (e.g., vaccination, pollution). Incentives can form PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, or pure coordination over prevention efforts.

Risk subcategory: Factually incorrect content (inaccuracies and fabricated sources)
Description: "One of the most vexing problems associated with AI models is that they occasionally present false information as if it is factual—often with authoritative-sounding text and fabricated quotes and sources. This unpredictable phenomenon of generating false information is well known to AI researchers, who have termed such erroneous output with the euphemistic label “hallucination.” "
justification: Multiple actors (media, platforms, users) choose between quality vs degraded info. Individual gains from low-quality spread vs collective harm fit PD, Chicken, coordination and stag-hunt dynamics.

Description: "Opacity surrounding the technical, internal decision-making processes of generative AI models is popularly known as the “black box problem.”277 Generative AI models, most ubiquitously built on deep neural networks with hundreds of billions of internal connections,278 have become so complex that their internal decision-making processes are no longer traceable or interpretable to even the most advanced expert observers. This means that, while the inputs and outputs of a system can be observed, developers cannot explain in detail why specific inputs correspond to specific outputs."
justification: Firms/clients choose to exploit or pay fair wages; each gains by exploiting if others don’t, but mutual exploitation can trigger backlash or regulation. PD, Chicken, and Stag Hunt all plausibly capture these incentives.

Description: "Opacity is not solely due to the technological complexity that limits developers’ and users’ understanding of how generative models function on a technical level. It is further exacerbated by the practices of organizations and companies that are advancing the field. Many are private companies that choose to withhold from the public many of the precise characteristics of their most advanced models."
justification: Risk is framed as aggregate deterioration of human capacities, not as strategic choices between interdependent actors with payoffs depending on others’ actions.

Description: "Beyond the inherent risks associated with the technical characteristics of the technology, numerous additional risks emerge from the potential applications that technology enables. The deployment of AI by more or less well-intentioned individuals presents significant societal threats, several of which are outlined below. As the technology advances and its capabilities expand, these risks intensify."
justification: Described as degradation from malfunction/overuse/under-staffing, i.e., a single-provider quality/reliability issue. No explicit strategic interaction between multiple actors is specified.

Description: "The advanced capabilities and widespread availability of generative AI models make it possible for malicious actors to conduct harmful activities with great efficiency and on a large scale, simultaneously reducing their operational costs. Cybercriminals can “jailbreak” AI tools to generate sensitive and harmful content. They can also exploit generative AI models to create content that is persuasive and tailored to a targeted individual."
justification: Multiple actors: govts, firms, workers, platforms. Each’s choices (regulate, automate, organize, spread/counter disinfo) affect others’ payoffs. Policy vs profit vs stability maps to PD, Chicken, coordination, Stag Hunt.

Description: "Generative AI can help amplify the frequency and destructiveness of cyberattacks.311 It has the capacity “to increase the accessibility, success rate, scale, speed, stealth, and potency of cyberattacks. It enables the identification of critical vulnerabilities within targeted systems, facilitates the increase of the scale of cyberattacks, and accelerates the process by discovering innovative methods of system infiltration. Cyberattacks can inflict significant damage and may impact critical infrastructure, including electrical grids, financial systems, and weapons management systems."
justification: States or groups choose to develop/use tech for cyber or WMD attacks vs restraint. Payoffs depend on others’ armament, defense, retaliation—fitting PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, and adversarial Matching Pennies dynamics.

Description: "Many fear that generative AI could make the creation of biological weapons easier by providing access to critical knowledge and automated assistance to a wider range of actors to engage in malicious activities."
justification: Multiple actors (parties, platforms, states, voters) strategically manipulate or defend beliefs and institutions; payoffs depend on others’ moves, fitting conflict, coordination, and misinfo arms-race games.

Risk subcategory: Malicious use and abuse (sexually explicit content generation)
Description: "An illustrative case of malicious use of generative AI models is the creation of explicit sexual images. Generative AI technologies can be employed to produce deepfakes—for instance, superimposing a celebrity’s face onto the body of a performer in an adult film."
justification: States/operators choose investment/defense vs underinvestment or attack. Payoffs depend on others’ protections and targeting. This yields PD (underinvest), Chicken (escalation), coordination and stag-hunt structures.

Description: "Generative AI facilitates the automation of data analysis, offering numerous benefits, such as increased speed and the ability to process large volumes of information efficiently. Such ability significantly reduces the costs of processing unprecedented amounts of data quickly and simplifies the analysis of large-scale data related to individuals’ behaviors and beliefs. Moreover, it enhances the capability to analyze both textual and visual communications efficiently. Consequently, generative AI models improve the efficiency of real-time monitoring and censorship of social media content."
justification: Economic instability from tech use often stems from many actors (firms, funds, states) whose risk-taking and hedging affect each other, creating PD-style overrisking, Chicken-style brinkmanship, and stag-hunt/coordination on standards.

Description: "The advancement of AI for military purposes is rapidly ushering in a new phase of growth in military technology. Lethal Autonomous Weapons Systems (LAWS) possess the capability to detect, engage, and eliminate human targets independently, without human input.341 In 2020, a sophisticated AI agent surpassed experienced F-16 pilots in multiple simulated aerial combat scenarios, notably achieving a 5-0 victory against a human pilot through “aggressive and precise maneuvers” that the human could not surpass.342 Additionally, fully autonomous drones are already operational."
justification: Multiple actors: parties/campaigns/platforms trade off truthful vs manipulative content. Payoffs depend on others’ choices; can resemble PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, or adversarial Matching Pennies spin/counter-spin.

Description: "IIl-intentioned individuals or entities may deliberately use generative AI models to produce and spread disinformation—false or misleading information knowingly presented as if true—on a massive scale. In addition to increasing the scale and reach of disinformation, generative AI can create more convincing and targeted disinformation."
justification: Multiple actors (parties, groups, states) choose polarising vs cooperative tech/policy strategies; others’ moves affect payoffs. Captures as PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, and coordination/standard-setting games.

Risk subcategory: Bias and discrimination (bias in training datasets)
Description: "AI experts consider training data to be the most salient source of bias in generative AI models. For example, GPT- 2’s training data comes from outbound links from Reddit, a social network often criticized for hosting anti-feminist content.351 As a result, AI models trained on such data are more likely to produce outputs that reflect these biases."
justification: Parties (campaigns, platforms, adversaries) strategically choose to use or resist manipulative micro-targeting; payoffs depend on others’ moves. Can be modeled as PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, or zero-sum info war (Matching Pennies).

Description: "Generative AI models may also be subject to the “value embedding” phenomenon.361 “Value embedding” refers to the fact that developers of generative AI models strive to minimize biased outputs by retraining their models based on normative values.362 Contemporary state-of- the-art models not only reflect the values embedded within their training data, they also undergo additional fine-tuning that follows a set of chosen rules and principles. Due to the absence of universally accepted standards, developers bear the responsibility of making decisions on sensitive issues. These practices lead to concerns that a developer’s ideology and vision of the world are embedded in the model. This generates a risk that the model incorporates values that are either unrepresentative of certain segments of the population or that offer a static, oversimplified reflection of global cultural norms and evolving social views."
justification: Multiple actors (states, firms, communities) choose between conservation vs expansion. Each’s payoff depends on others’ choices, creating social-dilemma structures like PD/Chicken/Stag Hunt around habitat protection.

Risk subcategory: Bias and discrimination (value lock and outcome homogenization)
Description: "Because models are not necessarily retrained to reflect evolving societal views, language models risk “value lock- ins,” which “reifies older, less inclusive understandings.”370 Therefore, the continued use of outdated models may limit the presentation or exploration of alternative perspectives. Moreover, the deployment of identical foundation models by various downstream deployers poses a risk of “outcome homogenization,” creating a potential for homogeneity of bias across broad swathes of society. Identical and widely deployed models with prejudicial training datasets could further entrench existing biases in society. This phenomenon, in turn, has the potential to “institutionalize systemic exclusion and reinforce existing social hierarchies.”
justification: Multiple emitters/states choose emission levels; each gains from emitting while others restrain, but all are harmed by high total emissions. Classic PD; also fits stag hunt, chicken, and generic coordination on mitigation.

Risk subcategory: Influence, overreliance and dependence (influence and manipulation)
Description: "Despite the widely recognized potential of generative AI tools to “hallucinate” or produce harmful content, such tools can exert a noteworthy influence on the humans who engage with them. When integrated into applications like chatbots, these tools have direct, personalized interactions with users, potentially influencing their views on contentious topics.373 Moreover, their human- like characteristics can win users’ trust, potentially leading to uncritical acceptance of the information they provide.374 Interactions with these seemingly human- like AI models may also encourage users to share more personal information, enabling even more targeted content."
justification: The item just defines what counts as electronic waste; no concrete actors, choices, or interdependent payoffs are specified, so no strategic game-theoretic structure is present.

Description: "Beyond being simply influenced, humans may become overreliant on generative AI. Researchers with Microsoft’s AETHER (AI Ethics and Effects in Engineering and Research) define overreliance as users “accepting incorrect AI recommendations” or “making errors of commission” because they are “unable to determine whether or how much they should trust the AI.”
justification: Multiple actors (firms, regions) overuse shared energy: each gains from own high use but risks shortages. Interdependent payoffs => PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, coordination on conservation/standards all plausible.

Description: "Humans might become dependent on generative AI tools in ways similar to their emotional dependence on other technologies, such as smartphones or social networks."
justification: Producers/governments vs communities: each can invest in recycling/regulation or dump cheaply. Mutual restraint best, but each tempted to free‑ride, risking overuse—fits PD, Chicken, and Stag Hunt structures.

Description: "Traditionally, AI tools have been viewed as passive instruments controlled by users to achieve their goals, lacking the ability to take action or assume responsibilities. However, advanced AI tools are increasingly capable of taking initiative, operating independently of human control, and actively working toward optimal outcomes, even in uncertain situations."
justification: Data centres vs local communities/businesses: each can conserve or overuse water. Individual incentive to overuse but mutual overuse risks shortages or restrictions, fitting PD/Chicken/Stag Hunt structures.

Description: "As large models undergo scaling, they meet critical thresholds at which they spontaneously develop new capabilities. The term “emergent behavior” refers to the unexpected or surprising outputs such models can generate. Some of these new skills are definitely high risk, such as models’ ability to deceive, use their own strategies, seek power, autonomously replicate, and adapt or “self-exfiltrate.”"
justification: Risk concerns a single system’s automation level and its technical reliability/safety. No clear multi-actor strategic interaction or interdependent payoffs is specified.

Description: "Since the release of ChatGPT, significant discourse has emerged regarding the unprecedented legal challenges posed by generative AI systems. These challenges primarily involve protecting privacy and personal data, as well as preserving copyrights. The former encompasses safeguarding personal information, while the latter includes issues related to the use of copyrighted content for training AI models and determining the legal status of works produced by AI systems."
justification: Risk concerns a single actor’s diligence in choosing trustworthy data sources; no explicit strategic interaction or interdependent payoffs between multiple agents is specified.

Risk subcategory: Privacy and data collection concerns (collecting personal information or personally identifiable information)
Description: "Generative AI developers train their models with extensive datasets often gathered through online web scraping of websites that may include personal data or personally identifiable information (PII). For most generative AI applications, such as initial model training, the primary concerns are the quantity, variety, and quality of the data, not whether they include personally identifiable information. However, some web-scraped datasets may inadvertently include personal data. Additionally, when downstream developers integrate generative AI into their products or services by fine- tuning a pre-trained model, they often use their own in-house data, which may include personal information."
justification: Risk is about internal data understanding and quality within an AI project. It’s a single-actor competence/quality issue without strategic interaction between distinct actors.

Risk subcategory: Privacy and data collection concerns (data protection concerns)
Description: "The incorporation of personal data within training datasets raises numerous concerns. The primary issue is that personal data may be incorporated without the knowledge or consent of the individuals concerned, even though the data may include names, identification numbers, Social Security numbers, or other personal information. Another particularly difficult problem is related to the fact that complex models may “memorize” (i.e., store) specific threads of training data and regurgitate them when responding to a prompt.498 This data memorization can directly lead to leakage of personal data. Even if generative AI models do not memorize or leak personal data, they make it possible to recognize patterns or information structures that could enable malicious users to uncover personal details."
justification: Discriminative data bias here is mainly a single-actor data/ML quality issue; no explicit interacting agents with strategic choices and payoff interdependence are specified.

Risk subcategory: Copyright challenges (training models using copyrighted output)
Description: "Generative AI companies are regularly accused of violating copyright law by training AI models on copyrighted works without gaining permission or paying compensation to the copyright owners. In fact, a substantial number of copyrighted documents and books have been incorporated into the training datasets of generative AI models."
justification: As stated, this is a generic privacy harm from using personal data, with no explicit second actor or strategic interdependence; it is a compliance/quality issue, not a multi-actor game.

Description: "Even though models generally create new outputs, it is possible that the content produced by a generative AI tool—such as an image, or even computer code— could turn out to be almost identical to that used in the training data. Given that generative AI models tend to memorize fragments of their training data, they might reproduce these fragments, potentially leading to charges of copyright infringement."
justification: Risk is about incorrect/missing labels degrading learning quality. It’s a single-actor data quality/reliability issue; no strategic interdependence among actors is specified.

Risk subcategory: Copyright challenges (uncertain intellectual property status of AI-generated content)
Description: "The question of who owns the intellectual property rights associated with the output of an AI model remains unresolved in most legal systems. For now, it could be considered that the individual writing the prompt owns the resulting output—provided that there is sufficient human contribution."
justification: Attacker decides to poison or not; defender chooses costly defenses. Payoffs depend on each other’s choices (costs vs compromise). This can be modeled as PD (protect vs save cost) or adversarial Matching Pennies.

Description: "Beyond the risks associated with AI technology and its applications, and the legal challenges arising from its development, it is crucial to consider other long- term issues posed by the deployment of increasingly advanced generative AI models. These risks to society, sometimes referred to as “systemic risks,”537 encompass several key areas: the potential for excessive market concentration, the impacts on employment, environmental consequences, and broader risks to humanity."
justification: Issue is a single-actor data quality/reliability problem: matching train and operational distributions. No opposing or interdependent strategic actors, so no 2x2 game structure applies.

Risk subcategory: Concentration of market power (Trend toward market concentration)
Description: "In the generative AI market, barriers to entry are very high. Developers need access to vast volumes of data, computational resources, technical expertise, and capital. Large technology companies with such access are able to exploit economies of scale, economies of scope, and feedback effects (learning effects from user- generated data).542 All this gives them an overwhelming advantage over smaller companies, making competition increasingly challenging for these smaller entities."
justification: Risk is about single-actor technical quality of synthetic data vs real data and its impact on generalization. No explicit multi-actor strategic interaction or incentive coupling is specified.

Risk subcategory: Concentration of market power (Negative effects of increased market concentration)
Description: "The concentration of AI assets—encompassing data, hardware, and expertise—within a small group of global tech firms raises many concerns.564 Such a situation may stifle healthy competition, impede innovation, and potentially result in elevated costs for accessing AI technologies. Firms with control over essential resources for developing AI models may restrict access to these resources to prevent competition. For instance, if, in the future, training AI models increasingly relies on proprietary data, smaller organizations lacking access to such data might encounter significant barriers to entry and growth."
justification: Risk is mainly a single-actor quality/testing failure: misusing the test set degrades evaluation integrity, but there is no clear opposing actor with interdependent strategic choices.

Risk subcategory: Impact on labor markets (job loss and displacement)
Description: "Currently, a significant share of workers (three in five) worry about losing their jobs entirely to AI in the next 10 years—particularly those who already work with AI. Some studies conclude that AI tools (generative and non-generative) will create significant job losses.573 The OECD has found that occupations at highest risk of being lost to automation from AI account for about 27% of employment.5"
justification: Risk concerns a single developer’s design quality causing bias/unreliability. No explicit second actor or strategic interdependence of payoffs; thus it’s a pure single-actor quality failure, not a game.

Description: "AI is more likely to displace workers when it is designed to replicate human skills and intelligence.597 In such cases, there is a risk of concentrating wealth and power in the hands of a few individuals or organizations that control the capital. In addition, ordinary people, including those with significant expertise, may become less valued because machines would be performing their roles. This shift could lower wages, reduce the value of human work, and exacerbate economic inequality."
justification: Over- and underfitting are single-model technical reliability issues. No clear multi-actor strategic interaction or payoff interdependence is specified, so game-theoretic modeling is not central here.

Description: "Training large AI models requires a substantial amount of computing power to handle vast datasets, which translates into high energy consumption."
justification: Risk concerns single-actor quality/technical opacity reducing ability to detect shortcomings. No clear multi-actor strategic incentives or interdependent choices are specified.

Description: "Data centers use water for cooling to prevent servers from overheating. The water consumption associated with AI training and inference processes can be substantial, impacting local water resources."
justification: Corner-case unreliability is a technical robustness issue of one AI system facing rare inputs; no strategic interaction or interdependent payoffs between multiple actors is specified.

Risk subcategory: Artificial general intelligence (existential risk posed by Artificial General Intelligence)
Description: "In a paper called “How Does Artificial Intelligence Pose an Existential Risk?” published in 2017, Karina Vold and Daniel Harris suggested that humans might create a super-intelligent machine that could outsmart all other intelligences, remain beyond human control, and potentially engage in actions that are contrary to human interests.635 The prevailing narrative surrounding AI existential risk typically lies in the possibility of developing “Artificial General Intelligence” (AGI), or artificial super- intelligence (ASI)."
justification: Lack of robustness is a single-system technical reliability issue; no explicit multiple actors or interdependent strategic choices are specified, so no 2x2 game structure applies.

Description:
"Eased access to or synthesis of materially nefarious 
information or design capabilities related to chemical, biological, radiological, or nuclear (CBRN) weapons or other dangerous materials or agents."
justification: Risk is a single-actor technical reliability/validation issue (mismatch between test and operational data); no strategic interaction or interdependent payoffs between multiple actors.

Description: "The production of confidently stated but erroneous or false content (known colloquially as “hallucinations” or “fabrications”) by which users may be misled or deceived."
justification: Lifecycle stage taxonomy describes when hazards arise (data prep, modeling, operation), not strategic behavior between actors. This is a single-actor quality/engineering framing, so no game-theoretic core.

Description:
"Eased production of and access to violent, inciting, 
radicalizing, or threatening content as well as recommendations to carry out self-harm or 
conduct illegal activities. Includes difficulty controlling public exposure to hateful and disparaging or stereotyping content."
justification: Risk item only specifies a modeling lifecycle stage with no described actors, incentives, or interdependent choices, so no strategic interaction can be inferred.

Description: "Impacts due to leakage and unauthorized use, disclosure, or de-anonymization of biometric, health, location, or other personally identifiable information or sensitive data."
justification: Item is a taxonomic definition of hazard modes (technological, socio-technological, procedural); no concrete actors, choices, or interdependent payoffs are specified.

Description: "Impacts due to high compute resource utilization in training or operating GAI models, and related outcomes that may adversely impact ecosystems."
justification: Risk focuses on individual developers making poor model design choices and needing documentation; it’s a single-actor quality/process issue with no explicit strategic interaction between multiple agents.

Description: "Amplification and exacerbation of historical, societal, and systemic biases; performance disparities8 between sub-groups or languages, possibly due to non-representative training data, that result in discrimination, amplification of biases, or incorrect presumptions about performance; undesired homogeneity that skews system or model outputs, which may be erroneous, lead to ill-founded decision-making, or amplify harmful biases."
justification: Item only describes who is responsible at different system levels, not strategic choices or interdependent payoffs between actors. Thus no genuine multi-actor game structure applies.

Description:
"Arrangement s of or interactions between a human and an AI system 
which can result in the human inappropriately anthropomorphizing GAI systems or experiencing algorithmic aversion, automation bias, over-reliance, or emotional entanglement with GAI 
systems."
justification: Multiple actors (malicious vs defenders/public) with payoffs depending on others’ choices. Strategic tradeoffs in manipulating vs resisting; can be PD/Chicken-style or adversarial like Matching Pennies.

Description: "Lowered barrier to entry to generate and support the exchange and consumption of content which may not distinguish fact from opinion or fiction or acknowledge uncertainties, or could be leveraged for large-scale dis- and mis-information campaigns."
justification: Item is an unspecified malfunction risk with no described actors or strategic interaction; fits a pure technical/quality failure category, so no 2x2 game structure applies.

Description:
"Lowered barriers for offensive cyber capabilities, including via automated discovery and exploitation of vulnerabilities to ease hacking, malware, phishing, offensive cyber operations, or other cyberattacks; increased attack surface for targeted cyberattacks, which may compromise a system’s availability or the confidentiality or integrity of training data, code, or 
model weights."
justification: Item is a broad definitional category for systemic risks, not a specific interaction with defined actors or strategies, so no concrete multi-actor game-theoretic structure is specified.

Description: "Eased production or replication of alleged copyrighted, trademarked, or licensed content without authorization (possibly in situations which do not fall under fair use); eased exposure of trade secrets; or plagiarism or illegal replication."
justification: Multiple actors (AI labs, data centers, regulators) choose compute and efficiency levels; each affects emissions and others’ costs and reputation. Incentives to overuse vs restrain compute fit PD, Chicken, and Stag Hunt structures.

Description:
"Eased production of and access to obscene, 
degrading, and/or abusive imagery which can cause harm, including synthetic child sexual abuse material (CSAM), and nonconsensual intimate images (NCII) of adults."
justification: Item is only labeled 'nan' with no described actors, incentives, or interactions, so no strategic structure can be inferred; cannot model as any 2x2 game under the rules.

Description:
"Non-transparent or untraceable integration of 
upstream third-party components, including data that has been improperly obtained or not 
processed and cleaned due to increased automation from GAI; improper supplier vetting across the AI lifecycle; or other issues that diminish transparency or accountability for downstream 
users."
justification: Multiple actors (firms, workers, regulators) interact strategically over AI deployment, labor use, and rules. Incentives can form PD, coordination, and distributional/zero-sum conflicts, so many 2x2 structures apply.

Description: "As general- purpose AI covers a broad set of knowledge areas, it can be repurposed for malicious ends, potentially causing widespread harm. This section discusses some of the major risks of malicious use, but there are others and new risks may continue to emerge. While the risks discussed in this section range widely in terms of how well- evidenced they are, and in some cases, there is evidence suggesting that they may currently not be serious risks at all, we include them to provide a comprehensive overview of the malicious use risks associated with general- purpose AI systems."
justification: Multiple actors (labs, regulators, industries) face incentives to exploit animals/ignore AI suffering vs invest in welfare. Mutual restraint best, but each gains by defecting; PD/Chicken/Stag-hunt structures apply.

Risk subcategory: Harm to individuals through fake content
Description: "General- purpose AI systems can be used to increase the scale and sophistication of scams and fraud, for example through general- purpose AI- enhanced ‘phishing’ attacks. General- purpose AI can be used to generate fake compromising content featuring individuals without their consent, posing threats to individual privacy and reputation."
justification: Multiple actors (states, platforms, propagandists, users) strategically shape information systems. Incentives over truth vs manipulation, standards, and narratives map to PD, coordination, conflict and zero-sum signaling games.

Risk subcategory: Disinformation and manipulation of public opinion
Description: "AI, particularly general- purpose AI, can be maliciously used for disinformation (351), which for the purpose of this report refers to false information that was generated or spread with the deliberate intent to mislead or deceive. General- purpose AI- generated text can be indistinguishable from genuine human- generated material (352, 353), and may already be disseminated at scale on social media (354). In addition, general- purpose AI systems can be used to not only generate text but also fully synthetic or misleadingly altered images, audio, and video content. General- purpose AI tools might be used to persuade and manipulate people, which could have serious implications for political processes. General- purpose AI systems can be used to generate highly persuasive content at scale. This could, for example, be used in a commercial setting for advertising, or during an election campaign to influence public opinion"
justification: States/firms choose how much AI power to amass/regulate. Each prefers dominance but fears others’ build-up; payoffs depend on rivals’ choices, yielding PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, and coordination-like structures.

Description: "General- purpose AI systems could uplift the cyber expertise of individuals, making it easier for malicious users to conduct effective cyber- attacks, as well as providing a tool that can be used in cyber defence. General- purpose AI systems can be used to automate and scale some types of cyber operations, such as social engineering attacks."
justification: Multiple states and actors choose on arms, cyber offense/defense, and escalation; each payoff depends on others’ choices. Arms races, deterrence, and cyber moves map to PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, coordination, and zero-sum subgames.

Description: "General- purpose AI systems could accelerate advances in a range of scientific endeavours, from training new scientists to enabling faster research workflows. While these capabilities could have numerous beneficial applications, some experts have expressed concern that they could be used for malicious purposes, especially if further capabilities are developed soon before appropriate countermeasures are put in place. There are two avenues by which general- purpose AI systems could, speculatively, facilitate malicious use in the life sciences: firstly by providing increased access to information and expertise relevant to malicious use, and secondly by increasing the ceiling of capabilities, which may enable the development of more harmful versions of existing threats or, eventually, lead to novel threats (404, 405)."
justification: Firms/governments choose automation vs protection; payoffs depend on others’ choices (race to automate vs labor stability). This supports PD/Chicken (races), Stag Hunt/Coordination (policy harmonization, safety nets).

Description: None provided.
