Risk category: Misuse tactics to compromise GenAI systems (Model integrity)
Misuse tactics to compromise GenAI systems (Model integrity)
Description: "Prompt Injections are a form of Adversarial Input that involve manipulating the text instructions given to a GenAI system (Liu et al., 2023). Prompt Injections exploit loopholes in a model’s architec- tures that have no separation between system instructions and user data to produce a harmful output (Perez and Ribeiro, 2022). While researchers may use similar techniques to test the robustness of GenAI models, malicious actors can also leverage them. For example, they might flood a model with manipulative prompts to cause denial-of-service attacks or to bypass an AI detection software."

Risk category: Misuse tactics to compromise GenAI systems (Model integrity)
Description: "Adversarial Inputs involve modifying individual input data to cause a model to malfunction. These modifications, which are often imperceptible to humans, exploit how the model makes decisions to produce errors (Wallace et al., 2019) and can be applied to text, but also to images, audio, or video (e.g. changing pixels in an image of a panda in a way that causes a model to label it as a gibbon).6"

Risk category: Misuse tactics to compromise GenAI systems (Model integrity)
Description: "Jailbreaking aims to bypass or remove restrictions and safety filters placed on a GenAI model completely (Chao et al., 2023; Shen et al., 2023). This gives the actor free rein to generate any output, regardless of its content being harmful, biassed, or offensive. All three of these are tactics that manipulate the model into producing harmful outputs against its design. The difference is that prompt injections and adversarial inputs usually seek to steer the model towards producing harmful or incorrect outputs from one query, whereas jailbreaking seeks to dismantle a model’s safety mechanisms in their entirety."

Risk category: Misuse tactics to compromise GenAI systems (Model integrity)
Description: "Model Diversion takes model manipulation one step further, by repurposing (often open-source) generative AI models in a way that diverts them from their intended functionality or from the use cases envisioned by their developers (Lin et al., 2024). An example of this is training the BERT open source model on the DarkWeb to create DarkBert.7"

Risk category: Misuse tactics to compromise GenAI systems (Model integrity)
Description: "Data Exfiltration goes beyond revealing private information, and involves illicitly obtaining the training data used to build a model that may be sensitive or proprietary. Model Extraction is the same attack, only directed at the model instead of the training data — it involves obtaining the architecture, parameters, or hyper-parameters of a proprietary model (Carlini et al., 2024)."

Risk category: Misuse tactics to compromise GenAI systems (Model integrity)
Description: "Steganography is the practice of hiding coded messages in GenAI model outputs, which may allow malicious actors to communicate covertly.8"

Risk category: Misuse tactics to compromise GenAI systems (Model integrity)
Description: "Data Poisoning involves deliberately corrupting a model’s training dataset to introduce vulnerabilities, derail its learning process, or cause it to make incorrect predictions (Carlini et al., 2023). For example, the tool Nightshade is a data poisoning tool, which allows artists to add invisible changes to the pixels in their art before uploading online, to break any models that use it for training.9 Such attacks exploit the fact that most GenAI models are trained on publicly available datasets like images and videos scraped from the web, which malicious actors can easily compromise."

Risk category: Misuse tactics to compromise GenAI systems (Data integrity)
Misuse tactics to compromise GenAI systems (Data integrity)
Description: "Privacy Compromise attacks reveal sensitive or private information that was used to train a model. For example, personally identifiable information or medical records."

Risk category: Misuse tactics to compromise GenAI systems (Data integrity)
Description: "Data Exfiltration goes beyond revealing private information, and involves illicitly obtaining the training data used to build a model that may be sensitive or proprietary. Model Extraction is the same attack, only directed at the model instead of the training data — it involves obtaining the architecture, parameters, or hyper-parameters of a proprietary model (Carlini et al., 2024)."

"Without accurate documentation on how a model's data was collected, curated, and used to train a model, it might be harder to satisfactorily explain the behavior of the model with respect to the data."

"Data provenance refers to tracing history of data, which includes its ownership, origin, and transformations. Without standardized and established methods for verifying where the data came from, there are no guarantees that the data is the same as the original source and has the correct usage terms."
"Laws and other restrictions can limit or prohibit the use of some data for specific AI use cases."
"Laws and other regulations might limit the collection of certain types of data for specific AI use cases."
"Laws and other restrictions can limit or prohibit transferring data."

"Inclusion or presence of personal identifiable information (PII) and sensitive personal information (SPI) in the data used for training or fine tuning the model might result in unwanted disclosure of that information."
"Existing laws could include providing data subject rights such as opt-out, right to access, and right to be forgotten."

"Even with the removal or personal identifiable information (PII) and sensitive personal information (SPI) from data, it might be possible to identify persons due to correlations to other features available in the data."
"Historical and societal biases that are present in the data are used to train and fine-tune the model."
"Terms of service, license compliance, or other IP issues may restrict the ability to use certain data for building models."
"Confidential information might be included as part of the data that is used to train or tune the model."

"Data contamination occurs when incorrect data is used for training. For example, data that is not aligned with model’s purpose or data that is already set aside for other development tasks such as testing and evaluation."
"Unrepresentative data occurs when the training or fine-tuning data is not sufficiently representative of the underlying population or does not measure the phenomenon of interest."
"Using undesirable output (for example, inaccurate, inappropriate, and user content) for retraining purposes can result in unexpected model behavior."
"Improper collection and preparation of training or tuning data includes data label errors and by using data with conflicting information or misinformation."
"A type of adversarial attack where an adversary or malicious insider injects intentionally corrupted, false, misleading, or incorrect samples into the training or fine-tuning datasets."
"A prompt injection attack forces a generative model that takes a prompt as input to produce unexpected output by manipulating the structure, instructions, or information contained in its prompt."

"An attribute inference attack is used to detect whether certain sensitive features can be inferred about individuals who participated in training a model. These attacks occur when an adversary has some prior knowledge about the training data and uses that knowledge to infer the sensitive data."
"Evasion attacks attempt to make a model output incorrect results by slightly perturbing the input data that is sent to the trained model."
"A prompt leak attack attempts to extract a model's system prompt (also known as the system message)."
"A jailbreaking attack attempts to break through the guardrails that are established in the model to perform restricted actions."

"Because generative models tend to produce output like the input provided, the model can be prompted to reveal specific kinds of information. For example, adding personal information in the prompt increases its likelihood of generating similar kinds of personal information in its output. If personal data was included as part of the model’s training, there is a possibility it could be revealed."

"A membership inference attack repeatedly queries a model to determine whether a given input was part of the model’s training. More specifically, given a trained model and a data sample, an attacker samples the input space, observing outputs to deduce whether that sample was part of the model's training."

"An attribute inference attack repeatedly queries a model to detect whether certain sensitive features can be inferred about individuals who participated in training a model. These attacks occur when an adversary has some prior knowledge about the training data and uses that knowledge to infer the sensitive data."
"Personal information or sensitive personal information that is included as a part of a prompt that is sent to the model."
"Confidential information might be included as a part of the prompt that is sent to the model."
"Copyrighted information or other intellectual property might be included as a part of the prompt that is sent to the model."

"Poor model accuracy occurs when a model’s performance is insufficient to the task it was designed for. Low accuracy might occur if the model is not correctly engineered, or there are changes to the model’s expected inputs."
"Content might not be clearly disclosed as AI generated."
"Improper usage occurs when a model is used for a purpose that it was not originally designed for."
"Generative AI models might be used intentionally to generate hateful, abusive, and profane (HAP) or obscene content."
"Generative AI models might be used with the sole intention of harming people."
"Generative AI models might be intentionally used to imitate people through deepfakes by using video, images, audio, or other modalities without their consent."
"Generative AI models might be used to intentionally create misleading or false information to deceive or influence a targeted audience."
"When a model provides advice without having enough information, resulting in possible harm if the advice is followed."
"Models might generate code that causes harm or unintentionally affects other systems."

"In AI-assisted decision-making tasks, reliance measures how much a person trusts (and potentially acts on) a model’s output. Over-reliance occurs when a person puts too much trust in a model, accepting a model’s output when the model’s output is likely incorrect. Under-reliance is the opposite, where the person doesn’t trust the model but should."
"Toxic output occurs when the model produces hateful, abusive, and profane (HAP) or obscene content. This also includes behaviors like bullying."
"A model might generate language that leads to physical harm The language might include overtly violent, covertly dangerous, or otherwise indirectly unsafe statements."
"A model might generate content that is similar or identical to existing work protected by copyright or covered by open-source license agreement."

"When confidential information is used in training data, fine-tuning data, or as part of the prompt, models might reveal that data in the generated output. Revealing confidential information is a type of data leakage."
"Without access to the training data, the types of explanations a model can provide are limited and more likely to be incorrect."
"The content of the training data used for generating the model’s output is not accessible."
"Explanations for model output decisions might be difficult, imprecise, or not possible to obtain."

"Source attribution is the AI system's ability to describe from what training data it generated a portion or all its output. Since current techniques are based on approximations, these attributions might be incorrect."

"Hallucinations generate factually inaccurate or untruthful content with respect to the model’s training data or input. This is also sometimes referred to lack of faithfulness or lack of groundedness."
"Generated content might unfairly represent certain groups or individuals."

"Decision bias occurs when one group is unfairly advantaged over another due to decisions of the model. This might be caused by biases in the data and also amplified as a result of the model’s training."

"When personal identifiable information (PII) or sensitive personal information (SPI) are used in training data, fine-tuning data, or as part of the prompt, models might reveal that data in the generated output. Revealing personal information is a type of data leakage."
"Terms of service, licenses, or other rules restrict the use of certain models."
"Determining who is responsible for an AI model is challenging without good documentation and governance processes."
"Legal uncertainty about the ownership and intellectual property rights of AI-generated content."
"Insufficient documentation of the system that uses the model and the model’s purpose within the system in which it is used."
"Testing is unrepresentative when the test inputs are mismatched with the inputs that are expected during deployment."

"Since foundation models can be used for many purposes, a model’s intended use is important for defining the relevant risks of that model. As the use changes, the relevant risks might correspondingly change."
"Lack of data transparency is due to insufficient documentation of training or tuning dataset details. "
"A metric selected to measure or track a risk is incorrectly selected, incompletely measuring the risk, or measuring the wrong risk for the given context."
"Lack of model transparency is due to insufficient documentation of the model design, development, and evaluation process and the absence of insights into the inner workings of the model."
"AI model risks are socio-technical, so their testing needs input from a broad set of disciplines and diverse testing practices."
"AI systems might overly represent certain cultures that result in a homogenization of culture and thoughts."
"Easy access to high-quality generative models might result in students that use AI models to plagiarize existing work intentionally or unintentionally."
"Widespread adoption of foundation model-based AI systems might lead to people's job loss as their work is automated if they are not reskilled."

"It is important to include the perspectives or concerns of communities that are affected by model outcomes when designing and building models. Failing to include these perspectives makes it difficult to understand the relevant context for the model and to engender trust within these communities."
"Easy access to high-quality generative models might result in students that use AI models to bypass the learning process."
"AI, and large generative models in particular, might produce increased carbon emissions and increase water usage for their training and operation."
"When workers who train AI models such as ghost workers are not provided with adequate working conditions, fair compensation, and good health care benefits that also include mental health."
"AI might affect the individuals’ ability to make choices and act independently in their best interests."

justification: Many AI actors over-deploy models, each gaining individually while jointly degrading societal commons. Strategic interdependence fits PD and stag-hunt style social dilemmas, plus general coordination on restraint.
Risk focuses on a single AI product being unintentionally harmful; described as a design/quality failure with no explicit strategic interaction among multiple actors.

Multiple actors (AI firms, regulators, society) interact; each can curb or ignore harms. Profit vs social cost yields PD-like, Chicken-like, or stag-hunt structures depending on regulation and expectations.

Criminals vs law enforcement (and public) have interdependent payoffs: levels of weaponization, investment in defense, deterrence, escalation. Modeled as PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, or adversarial Matching Pennies surveillance/evasion.
Bias, toxicity, and privacy leaks stem from model quality/reliability, not strategic interaction between actors; no interdependent choices or incentives → not game-theoretic.
Toxicity here is a single-actor content-quality issue of a model’s output, not a structured interaction where multiple agents’ payoffs depend on each other’s strategic choices.
Privacy leakage as stated is mainly a technical/quality failure (model outputs sensitive data) without explicit interacting strategic agents or payoff interdependence.
Hallucinated or inaccurate LLM outputs are a single-actor technical quality issue; no explicit second agent whose payoffs depend on strategic choices, so no 2x2 game structure applies.
Factuality errors are a single-system quality/reliability issue. No explicit second actor with strategic choices; no interdependent payoffs, so no 2x2 game structure fits.
Faithfulness errors are a single-system factuality/quality failure. No distinct actors with interdependent payoffs are specified, so no genuine strategic game structure applies.

Description is a broad value judgment about adverse social impacts from improper LLM use; no concrete actors, strategies, or interdependent payoffs are specified, so no clear game-theoretic structure.
Described mainly as a technical/compliance issue: LLM output resembles copyrighted works. No explicit interacting decision-makers or strategic responses are specified.

Hackers and defenders choose to deploy/defend LLM-based attacks; outcomes depend on each other’s strategies. This can be modeled as PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt (risk vs safety) or Matching Pennies (attack vs defense patterns).
Python interpreter vulnerabilities are primarily a technical reliability/quality issue; no clear multi-actor strategic incentives or interdependent choices are specified.

Multiple actors (labs, regulators, malicious users) choose between safety vs racing/open access. Incentives interdependent: PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, coordination on standards, and some win-win oversight structures all apply.

Multiple actors—labs, oversight bodies, possibly AIs—interact over value selection, honesty vs. deception, and reward signals. Incentives can be conflictual (PD, Chicken, Matching Pennies) or coordination/assurance (BoS, Stag Hunt, Coordination).

Multiple actors (AI firms, rights holders, regulators) choose data/use policies; payoffs depend on others’ choices. Conflicts over standards and enforcement resemble PD, stag hunt, and coordination/Bach-or-Stravinski.

Humans vs AGI: humans choose control measures, AGI chooses compliance or escape. Each side’s best response depends on the other’s move, with PD/Chicken/Stag-Hunt structures over control vs autonomy and risk.

Multiple actors choose whether to adopt AI tools. Collective restraint reduces inequality, but each benefits by adopting. Strategic complementarity and risk of being left behind → coordination/stag-hunt.

Governments, platforms, and political groups strategically choose surveillance, nudging, or restraint. Incentives create dilemmas, brinkmanship, and security races fitting PD, Chicken, Stag Hunt, and adversarial Matching Pennies.
This is a high-level fairness/value principle with no explicit actors, choices, or interdependent payoffs described, so it’s not a concrete multi-actor strategic scenario for 2x2 game modeling.
Misapplication here is primarily a single-actor misuse of a tool; description lacks explicit interacting agents with interdependent incentives, so no clear 2x2 strategic structure is specified.
Design failure risk is about internal system design quality/errors, not strategic interaction between multiple actors with interdependent payoffs; thus it’s a single-actor technical/quality issue.

User decides disclosure; attacker chooses to exploit. Each payoff depends on the other’s move. Privacy tradeoffs (sharing vs safety, attack vs abstain) can be PD-, Chicken-, or Stag-hunt-like depending on costs and incentives.

Multiple actors (firms, regulators, users) choose ML use levels; each payoff depends on others via shared environment. Overuse of harmful ML resembles PD/Chicken/Stag Hunt/Coordination on environmental protection.

As stated, this is a generic organizational downside (financial/reputational loss) without specified strategic counterparties or interdependent choices, so it’s a single-actor quality/impact risk, not a game.
Risk focuses on LM quality and bias harms from training data and performance gaps, not on strategic interaction between actors with interdependent payoffs.
This risk is about biased training data and resulting stereotypes, a single-system quality failure. No clear strategic interaction with payoffs between multiple actors is defined.
Risk is framed as a single-model quality/content failure (toxic generation), not as strategic interaction between multiple actors with interdependent payoffs.
Lower performance for some languages is mainly a single-actor technical/training data quality issue; no clear strategic interaction or payoff interdependence between distinct actors is specified.

At least two actors: LM provider vs affected parties/regulators. Provider can invest in privacy safeguards or not; others set/use policies. Interdependent payoffs fit PD (costly protection vs leakage) or stag hunt (coordination on strong safeguards).

Data holder and data subject (and possibly regulator) have interdependent choices: using LMs to infer traits vs protecting privacy. Can be framed as PD (exploit vs protect), stag hunt/coordination, or cooperative no-conflict.

Core risk is LM hallucination/low-quality output absent malicious intent; harms emerge without strategic interaction between distinct actors, so it is a single-actor technical reliability/quality issue.

Risk is mainly single-actor exposure to incorrect model outputs (hallucinations/misinformation). No second strategic actor whose payoff depends on the user’s choice, so no true game-theoretic interaction.

Malicious LM use involves attackers vs defenders; each side’s payoff depends on others’ defenses/attacks. Can be PD (arms race), Chicken (escalation risk), or Matching Pennies (adaptive attack–defend).

Multiple actors: disinformation producers vs defenders/platforms/public. Each side’s payoff depends on the other’s investment in LM tools and countermeasures, yielding conflict, brinkmanship, and zero-sum aspects.
